proposals.title: Pathogen-Specific Disparities in Outcomes and Healthcare Utilization for Infective Endocarditis in People Who Inject Drugs: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Infective endocarditis (IE) related to injection drug use (IDU) is a critical clinical and public health challenge, with rising incidence in the pre-2026 era. While Staphylococcus aureus is known to dominate IDU-IE, a detailed, pathogen-specific analysis of how causative organisms differentially drive mortality, readmission, and healthcare costs in IDU-IE versus non-IDU-IE is lacking. Such an analysis is essential for risk stratification, resource allocation, and designing targeted interventions for this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between specific pathogens (e.g., S. aureus, MRSA, MSSA, streptococci, enterococci) and adverse outcomes (mortality, readmission, cost) is significantly stronger in patients with IDU-IE compared to patients with non-IDU-IE, independent of baseline comorbidities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study of adults hospitalized with IE from 2015-2024, identified via ICD-10 codes. The cohort will be stratified by IDU status (using validated criteria) and causative pathogen from blood cultures. Primary outcomes will be 90-day all-cause mortality, 30-day all-cause readmission, and index hospitalization costs. We will use multivariable logistic regression and generalized linear models, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities (Charlson Index), and severity of illness, to test for interaction effects between IDU status and pathogen on each outcome. Sensitivity analyses will account for recurrent episodes and treatment factors like definitive surgery.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence of pathogen-outcome effect modification by IDU status, identifying which organisms pose the greatest excess risk in this population. It will inform clinical prognostication and highlight targets for pathogen-specific prevention and care coordination programs. The cost analysis will offer crucial data for health systems managing the economic burden of the IDU-IE epidemic.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates IDU and S. aureus with worse IE outcomes, this study uniquely interrogates the interaction, quantifying how the risk conferred by *specific pathogens* differs fundamentally for people who inject drugs, moving beyond simple comparisons of IDU vs. non-IDU cohorts.

proposals.title: Effectiveness of the SHIELD OC Decolonization Protocol on Reducing Multidrug-Resistant Organism Prevalence in a Multi-Facility Collaborative: A Pragmatic, Stepped-Wedge Cluster Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: Multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs), including MRSA, are a persistent threat in healthcare settings, leading to increased morbidity, mortality, and cost. Nursing homes and long-term care facilities are critical reservoirs for MDRO transmission. While decolonization with chlorhexidine and nasal antiseptics has shown efficacy in targeted trials, its real-world effectiveness when implemented across a heterogeneous collaborative of facilities remains poorly understood, particularly in the post-COVID-19 era where infection control practices have been stressed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the systematic implementation of the SHIELD OC decolonization protocol (routine chlorhexidine bathing and nasal iodophor) across a collaborative of nursing homes will lead to a significant and sustained reduction in the pooled prevalence of target MDROs (MRSA, ESBL-producing organisms) among residents compared to baseline standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across the SHIELD OC collaborative. All participating facilities will begin in a control phase (standard care). At randomly assigned times, each facility will cross over to the intervention phase, implementing the SHIELD OC protocol (daily chlorhexidine gluconate bathing and twice-weekly nasal iodophor for all residents). The primary outcome is the monthly point prevalence of combined target MDRO colonization, measured via serial nares and inguinal/perirectal surveillance cultures from a random sample of residents. We will use generalized linear mixed models with facility-level random effects to analyze the longitudinal prevalence data, adjusting for time trends, facility characteristics, and baseline colonization pressure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-quality, real-world evidence on the population-level effectiveness of a decolonization bundle in a vulnerable nursing home population. It will directly inform infection prevention policy for large collaboratives and health systems. Furthermore, it will generate data on the feasibility and durability of decolonization effects in routine practice, beyond highly controlled trial settings.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond single-facility or highly controlled efficacy trials to evaluate the pragmatic, scaled implementation of a decolonization protocol across an entire collaborative, a crucial step for understanding its public health impact. It also integrates this intervention research within the established SHIELD OC framework, leveraging its existing infrastructure for surveillance and participant engagement.

proposals.title: Clinical Impact of Multi-Drug Resistant Organism Co-colonization on Subsequent Infection and Mortality: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The epidemiology of MDROs like MRSA, VRE, and resistant Gram-negatives is often studied in isolation, yet patients, especially in high-risk settings like nursing homes or post-COVID-19 hospitalization, are frequently colonized by multiple pathogens. Pre-2026 research has established single-pathogen colonization as a risk factor for subsequent infection, but the synergistic or additive risk posed by co-colonization is poorly understood, hindering targeted decolonization and infection prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-colonization with two or more MDROs is associated with a significantly higher risk of developing a subsequent MDRO infection and all-cause mortality compared to colonization with a single MDRO or no MDRO.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked clinical and microbiological data from adult participants previously hospitalized for COVID-19 pneumonia, a population at high risk for MDRO acquisition. Using routine surveillance culture data, we will define cohorts: no MDRO, single MDRO (e.g., MRSA only), and co-colonized (e.g., MRSA + a resistant Gram-negative). The primary outcome will be time to first MDRO infection (bacteremia, pneumonia, UTI). Secondary outcomes include 90-day all-cause mortality. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression, adjusting for associated risks like age, comorbidities, and ICU stay, to estimate hazard ratios for co-colonization.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimates of the incremental risk conferred by MDRO co-colonization in a post-COVID-19 cohort. It will inform clinical risk stratification, potentially justifying more aggressive decolonization protocols for co-colonized patients. The findings will add a critical dimension to the understanding of MDRO ecology and its direct clinical consequences.
proposals.novelty: While prior work, including Dr. McKinnell's, has examined the impact of decolonization on single-pathogen carriage, this study explicitly focuses on the clinical outcomes of concurrent colonization by multiple MDROs, a common but under-investigated clinical scenario. The analysis leverages a vulnerable, well-characterized post-COVID-19 population to explore this interaction.

proposals.title: Integrating Functional Status and Decolonization History into a Predictive Model for Multidrug-Resistant Organism Colonization in High-Risk Adults
proposals.motivation: Predictive models for MDRO colonization, crucial for infection prevention, have traditionally focused on basic demographics and prior antibiotic use. However, the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted how functional decline and immune dysregulation increase infection risk. Furthermore, the impact of prior participant engagement in decolonization protocols (e.g., chlorhexidine/iodophor) on subsequent colonization risk is poorly quantified, representing a critical gap in personalizing prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating detailed metrics of functional status (e.g., Activities of Daily Living scores) and structured histories of prior decolonization participation will significantly improve the accuracy of predictive models for MDRO colonization compared to models using traditional clinical factors alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of adults in post-acute and long-term care settings, building on Dr. McKinnell's prior work. Baseline assessment will include standardized functional status tools (e.g., Barthel Index), detailed decolonization history, and serial surveillance cultures for MRSA and other MDROs. We will employ multivariable logistic regression and machine learning algorithms (specifically, random forest and gradient boosting machines) to develop and validate a composite risk score. Model performance will be compared to a baseline model containing only traditional factors (e.g., prior hospitalization, comorbidities).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinically actionable risk stratification tool that moves beyond static factors to include dynamic patient-centered data. It will directly inform targeted decolonization strategies, potentially reducing unnecessary antimicrobial use and environmental contamination. The findings will advance the field of precision infection prevention by formally integrating functional and behavioral patient factors into risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While functional status is a known general health indicator, its specific integration into MDRO colonization models is novel. Explicitly modeling a patient's history as a 'participant' in prior decolonization interventions as a modifiable risk factor represents a new, decolonization-informed approach to predictive epidemiology.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Standard-of-Care Comparator for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) are a critical public health threat. Colistin is frequently used as the standard-of-care comparator in clinical trials for new anti-CRE agents, despite its known nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity, and the increasing availability of newer, potentially safer combination and targeted therapies. This practice may bias trial outcomes and fail to reflect evolving real-world treatment paradigms, potentially disadvantaging novel therapies or misguiding clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the clinical efficacy and safety profile of colistin-based regimens for CRE infections are not consistently superior to other available antibiotic regimens, and that its use as a universal comparator is not justified across all CRE infection types and patient populations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials and high-quality observational cohort studies (pre-2026) comparing colistin-based regimens to non-colistin-based regimens for the treatment of CRE infections. The primary outcome will be all-cause mortality. Secondary outcomes include clinical cure, microbiological eradication, and incidence of acute kidney injury. We will perform subgroup analyses by infection source (e.g., bacteremia, pneumonia, UTI), specific CRE genotype (e.g., KPC, NDM), and patient comorbidities. Statistical analysis will use random-effects models to calculate pooled risk ratios with 95% confidence intervals, assessing heterogeneity with I² statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive, evidence-based assessment of colistin's relative effectiveness and safety, challenging its default status as a trial comparator. It will inform the design of future antimicrobial trials for CRE by proposing context-specific comparator regimens. The findings will also offer guidance to clinicians on optimal treatment selection based on infection characteristics.
proposals.novelty: While individual studies have questioned colistin's efficacy, no pre-2026 synthesis has systematically critiqued its foundational role as the universal comparator in CRE trial design. This proposal directly challenges a methodological norm in antimicrobial development by applying rigorous epidemiological methods used in Dr. McKinnell's prior work on outcomes and colonization.

proposals.title: A Composite Endpoint Analysis of Clinical and Microbiological Outcomes in MRSA Bacteremia: Re-evaluating Trial Efficacy Beyond All-Cause Mortality
proposals.motivation: In antibiotic trials for serious infections like MRSA bacteremia, all-cause mortality is a robust but insensitive endpoint, requiring large sample sizes and potentially obscuring meaningful clinical benefit. This is particularly relevant for vulnerable populations, such as those in long-term care settings with high colonization rates, where decolonization and treatment strategies need more nuanced evaluation. A composite endpoint could improve trial feasibility and better capture patient-centered outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite endpoint of clinical cure (e.g., resolution of signs/symptoms) and microbiological eradication at a defined test-of-cure visit is a valid, sensitive, and patient-relevant primary efficacy measure for phase III trials evaluating treatments for MRSA bacteremia, and will demonstrate non-inferiority of novel regimens more efficiently than all-cause mortality alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of individual participant data from recent MRSA bacteremia trials (e.g., DOTS trial data if available) and observational cohorts. Using time-to-event (survival) analysis and competing risks regression, we will model the composite endpoint against mortality. We will assess the association between the composite endpoint and long-term outcomes (e.g., 90-day mortality, readmission) and calculate the sample size required for a trial using each endpoint to demonstrate non-inferiority.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological framework for designing more efficient antibiotic trials, potentially accelerating the evaluation of new therapies. It will generate evidence to support regulatory and clinical acceptance of composite endpoints in serious bacterial infections, moving beyond a sole reliance on mortality.
proposals.novelty: While composite endpoints are used in cardiology, their rigorous validation for regulatory-grade antibiotic trials in bacteremia, specifically integrating decolonization-era understandings of MRSA, is limited. This proposal directly challenges the prevailing mortality-centric efficacy paradigm in antimicrobial drug development.

proposals.title: A Decolonial Analysis of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Clinical Trial Populations: A Global Genomic and Epidemiological Discrepancy Study
proposals.motivation: Clinical trials for novel antimicrobials against CRE are often conducted in high-income countries, potentially failing to represent the global epidemiology of resistance. This lack of representativeness risks generating efficacy data that does not translate to regions with different strain prevalence, resistance mechanisms, and patient comorbidities, undermining global equity in infectious disease outcomes. This directly addresses a decolonization imperative in global health research by questioning whose microbial reality is centered in evidence generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the genomic and epidemiological profiles of CRE isolates and patients enrolled in pivotal international antimicrobial trials systematically differ from those found in global, real-world surveillance data, with underrepresentation of strains and resistance determinants prevalent in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs).
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-analysis and comparative genomic study. First, we will systematically extract and harmonize CRE isolate metadata (species, carbapenemase genes, sequence type) and patient comorbidity data from published Phase 2/3 trial registries and publications (pre-2026). This trial-derived dataset will be compared against a contemporaneous, global real-world dataset compiled from public repositories (e.g., NCBI Pathogen Detection, SENTRY surveillance). Discrepancy will be quantified using diversity indices (Shannon), chi-square tests for strain/mechanism distribution, and multivariate logistic regression to model the association between trial participation and geographic/epidemiological factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence of the gap between trial and real-world CRE epidemiology, offering a framework for evaluating trial generalizability. It will produce actionable recommendations for designing more inclusive, globally representative trials. The findings will advance decolonial clinical research by highlighting biases in participant and pathogen selection that perpetuate health inequities.
proposals.novelty: While trial generalizability is a known concern, applying a decolonial lens with a specific, quantitative genomic and comorbidity comparison for CRE is novel. It moves beyond anecdotal criticism to data-driven disparity metrics, linking clinical trial design directly to issues of microbial equity and antimicrobial resistance (AMR) justice.

proposals.title: Modeling the Impact of Interdependent Colonization Events on MRSA Infection Trajectories and Healthcare Costs in Nursing Home Populations
proposals.motivation: Current models for predicting healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) like MRSA often treat colonization and infection events as independent, ignoring the critical role of transmission dynamics and herd immunity within congregate settings. This limits the accuracy of cost projections and intervention planning, particularly in high-risk environments like nursing homes where McKinnell's prior work on decolonization has shown efficacy. A more realistic model is needed to inform resource allocation and infection prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating transmission dynamics and herd immunity effects into a model of MRSA colonization will significantly alter the predicted long-term infection rate and associated cost trajectory in a nursing home population, compared to a model assuming independent events.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based simulation model (ABM) using a modified SEIR (Susceptible-Exposed-Infectious-Recovered) framework, where 'Infectious' represents MRSA colonization. The model will be parameterized using pre-2026 data from McKinnell's nursing home decolonization trial (chlorhexidine/iodophor) and published transmission rates. We will compare the ABM's outputs against a standard independent-event (Poisson) model. Key analyses will include simulating the impact of targeted versus universal decolonization strategies on outbreak probability and projecting 5-year direct medical costs under each modeling paradigm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic modeling framework for HAI prediction that accounts for social-network and herd immunity effects in closed populations. It will generate more accurate estimates of the long-term clinical and economic value of decolonization interventions, directly informing infection prevention policy in resource-limited settings like nursing homes.
proposals.novelty: While transmission models exist for MRSA in hospitals, applying them to model the cost trajectory of infections in nursing homes, and formally testing the 'independence' assumption against a dynamic model, is a novel synthesis of clinical epidemiology, health economics, and operations research specific to this vulnerable population.

proposals.title: Time-Dependent Risk of Multidrug-Resistant Organism Acquisition in Hospitalized Adults: A Competing Risks Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current models for MDRO colonization risk, such as MRSA, often treat patient factors as static, ignoring the dynamic nature of hospitalization and treatment. This limits the precision of infection prevention strategies. Understanding how risk evolves with time since admission and antibiotic exposure is crucial for timely, targeted decolonization interventions, especially in the context of post-COVID-19 care where healthcare-associated infection risks remain elevated.
proposals.hypothesis: The hazard of acquiring a new MDRO colonization is not constant but varies significantly as a function of time since hospital admission and time since initiation of systemic antibiotic therapy, with the highest risk occurring within specific temporal windows.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult patients hospitalized for ≥72 hours, leveraging electronic health record data. The primary outcome is time to first new MDRO (MRSA, VRE, resistant Gram-negatives) positive clinical culture or surveillance swab. We will employ Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard models to account for the competing risk of discharge or death. Key time-dependent covariates will include days since admission and days since antibiotic exposure, modeled using restricted cubic splines to capture non-linear risk trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a dynamic, time-explicit risk model for MDRO acquisition, moving beyond static risk scores. It will identify critical temporal windows for heightened surveillance or pre-emptive decolonization, directly informing clinical trial design for targeted interventions. The findings will advance the field of hospital epidemiology by integrating temporal dynamics into risk prediction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by McKinnell and others has identified associated risk factors, this analysis explicitly models risk as a continuous, time-varying function of hospitalization and treatment, a methodological shift from standard logistic regression. It applies a competing risks framework specifically to the colonization process, which is often obscured by clinical infection outcomes.

proposals.title: Decolonizing Risk Stratification: A Site-Specific, Multi-Factorial Model for Predicting Clinical Outcomes in Patients with Colistin-Resistant Gram-Negative Rod Infections
proposals.motivation: Colistin is a last-line antibiotic for multidrug-resistant Gram-negative rods (GNRs). The prevailing clinical assumption is that all colistin-resistant (CR) GNRs confer a uniformly high mortality risk, potentially leading to uniform, aggressive treatment. However, this one-size-fits-all approach may overlook critical nuances in patient risk based on infection ecology, bacterial burden, and co-resistance patterns, leading to suboptimal resource allocation and patient outcomes. A decolonized, patient-centered risk model is needed to move beyond this monolithic threat assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The clinical threat posed by colistin-resistant GNRs is not uniform but is significantly differentiated by the primary infection site, quantitative bacterial load, and the co-presence of resistance to other last-line agents (e.g., carbapenems, tigecycline).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with culture-confirmed CR-GNR infections (e.g., *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, *Acinetobacter baumannii*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*). Primary outcome will be 30-day all-cause mortality. Using multivariable logistic regression, we will build and compare three nested risk models: a baseline model with demographics and comorbidities (Model 1), a model adding infection site (e.g., urinary vs. bloodstream vs. respiratory) (Model 2), and a full model adding quantitative culture data (e.g., CFU/mL, semi-quantitative scores) and co-resistance profiles to non-colistin last-line agents (Model 3). Model discrimination will be assessed using the C-statistic and calibration plots.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, stratified risk model for CR-GNR infections, challenging the monolithic 'high-risk' label. It will offer clinicians a practical tool to identify patients at genuinely highest risk for poor outcomes, guiding more precise antimicrobial therapy and infection control resource deployment. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship and decolonization strategies in high-burden settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior research associates CR-GNR with poor outcomes, no pre-2026 study has systematically deconstructed this risk by integrating specific infection site, quantitative microbiology, and comprehensive co-resistance data into a single predictive model, applying a decolonization lens to challenge a homogenizing clinical dogma.

proposals.title: Beyond Binary: A Multidimensional Analysis of Prior Antibiotic Exposure as a Risk Factor for MRSA Colonization and Infection in Post-COVID-19 Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Prior antibiotic exposure is a well-established risk factor for multidrug-resistant organism (MDRO) colonization and infection, but epidemiological models often reduce this complex exposure to a simple binary (yes/no) variable. This oversimplification may obscure critical dose-response relationships and the differential impacts of specific antibiotic classes, limiting the precision of risk prediction and the development of targeted stewardship interventions, particularly in vulnerable populations recovering from COVID-19.
proposals.hypothesis: Detailed metrics of antibiotic exposure—including cumulative duration, defined daily dose (DDD), and sequence of agents—will provide a significantly more accurate and clinically informative model for predicting subsequent MRSA colonization and infection risk than traditional binary or class-only variables.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adults hospitalized for COVID-19 pneumonia, leveraging detailed electronic health record data. Antibiotic exposures in the 90 days prior to an index MDRO screening culture will be characterized using multiple metrics: binary exposure, antibiotic class, treatment duration, cumulative DDD, and sequence via a transition matrix. We will use multivariable logistic regression and machine learning models (e.g., random forest or gradient boosting) to compare the predictive performance (using AUC-ROC) of models incorporating these detailed metrics against a baseline model using only a binary variable for prior antibiotic exposure. The primary outcome will be MRSA colonization; a secondary analysis will assess progression to MRSA bacteremia.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological framework for quantifying antibiotic exposure more precisely in clinical research. It will generate evidence to inform antimicrobial stewardship by identifying high-risk exposure patterns (e.g., specific sequences or dose thresholds) that disproportionately drive MRSA risk. The findings will directly address decolonization research by refining the identification of high-risk participants for targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has associated antibiotic use with MDRO risk, this study systematically compares the predictive utility of granular, multidimensional exposure metrics against the standard binary approach within a clinically relevant post-COVID-19 cohort, integrating methods from pharmacoepidemiology and machine learning.

proposals.title: Synergistic Risk for MRSA Colonization and Infection in Immunocompromised Hosts: A Multi-Cohort Analysis of Solid Organ Transplant Recipients and Antibiotic Exposure
proposals.motivation: Solid organ transplant recipients are at high risk for multidrug-resistant organism (MDRO) colonization and infection due to immunosuppression. Pre-2026 clinical research often treats risk factors like immunosuppression and antibiotic exposure as independent, additive variables, potentially missing critical synergistic interactions that exponentially increase risk. Understanding these interactions is crucial for developing targeted decolonization and stewardship strategies in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to specific antibiotic classes (e.g., fluoroquinolones, third-generation cephalosporins) and solid organ transplantation status interact synergistically, resulting in a risk for MRSA colonization and subsequent infection that is greater than the sum of their independent effects.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-cohort analysis merging data from prior studies on MDRO colonization in nursing homes and clinical trials of S. aureus bacteremia with a new cohort of transplant patients. Using multivariable logistic regression, we will model the main effects of transplantation and antibiotic exposure, and include an interaction term to test for synergy (effect modification). The primary outcome will be incident MRSA colonization or infection. We will calculate the relative excess risk due to interaction (RERI) and the attributable proportion due to interaction (AP) to quantify synergistic effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide quantitative evidence for synergistic risk, moving beyond additive models to inform more precise risk stratification. It will directly contribute to the design of decolonization protocols (e.g., targeted use of chlorhexidine or nasal iodophor) and antibiotic stewardship guidelines specifically for immunocompromised hosts. The findings will bridge research on colonization, clinical infection, and decolonization in a high-risk group.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by McKinnell and others has established independent risk factors and decolonization efficacy, this proposal explicitly tests for statistical interaction and synergy between a profound host factor (transplantation) and a key modifiable exposure (antibiotics), a non-obvious relationship not fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Causal Estimation of Prior Antibiotic Exposure on Subsequent Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) Infection Risk in Hospitalized Adults: A Propensity Score Matched Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic resistance, particularly from MRSA, remains a critical public health threat, exacerbated by antibiotic overuse. While prior antibiotic exposure is a known risk factor for subsequent resistant infections, observational studies are confounded by patient severity and indication bias. Pre-2026, robust causal inference methods are underutilized in clinical infectious disease research to isolate the true effect of antibiotic exposure from these confounding factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that after rigorously controlling for confounding using propensity score methods, prior exposure to specific antibiotic classes (e.g., fluoroquinolones, beta-lactams) will demonstrate a significant, causal increased risk for subsequent clinical MRSA infection in hospitalized adults, independent of underlying patient comorbidities and severity of illness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from adults hospitalized with bacterial infections. The primary exposure is prior systemic antibiotic use within 90 days pre-admission. The outcome is a clinical MRSA infection (e.g., bacteremia, pneumonia) during the index hospitalization. We will construct propensity scores using logistic regression with pre-exposure covariates including demographics, comorbidities, prior healthcare exposure, and severity markers. Exposed and unexposed patients will be matched 1:1 using nearest-neighbor matching without replacement (caliper = 0.2 SD of the logit of the propensity score). The average treatment effect on the treated (ATT) will be estimated using conditional logistic regression in the matched cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more rigorous, causally interpretable estimate of the effect of prior antibiotics on MRSA risk, moving beyond associative measures. It will demonstrate the application of robust causal inference techniques to a pressing clinical epidemiology question, offering a methodological template for similar studies in antimicrobial stewardship and decolonization research.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. McKinnell's prior work has identified associations and risks for MDROs, this study explicitly applies a formal causal inference framework (propensity score matching) to the question of antibiotic exposure and resistance, directly addressing confounding by indication—a major limitation in prior observational studies in this domain.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Framework to Infer Colistin Susceptibility from Low Resistance Risk Factors: A De-Escalation Tool for Gram-Negative Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Empiric use of colistin for suspected multidrug-resistant Gram-negative infections carries significant toxicity risks. De-escalation is critical but often delayed while awaiting susceptibility results. Pre-2026, risk prediction models focus on identifying resistance, not on inferring susceptibility when resistance risk is low, missing a key opportunity for earlier, safer antibiotic optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian model integrating patient-level colonization data, prior antibiotic exposure, and local epidemiology can accurately predict the probability of colistin susceptibility in Gram-negative bacteremia when traditional resistance risk scores are low, enabling safe de-escalation.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian logistic regression model using data from a retrospective cohort of adults with Gram-negative bacteremia (e.g., from McKinnell's prior COVID-19 and S. aureus studies). Features will include MDRO colonization status (informed by nursing home decolonization research), prior antibiotic use, healthcare exposure, and local susceptibility patterns. Model calibration will be assessed using Brier scores, and decision curve analysis will evaluate clinical utility for guiding de-escalation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel clinical decision-support tool that flips the paradigm from predicting resistance to inferring susceptibility, directly addressing antibiotic stewardship goals. It will offer a quantitative, probabilistic framework to guide earlier de-escalation from colistin, potentially reducing nephrotoxicity and collateral damage.
proposals.novelty: While risk scores for MDRO infection exist, this approach uniquely formulates susceptibility prediction as an inverse Bayesian inference problem, explicitly leveraging low-risk scenarios—a concept not prominent in pre-2026 antimicrobial stewardship models.

proposals.title: Decolonizing Resistance: A Case-Control Study of Patient and Care Factors Associated with the Absence of Colistin-Resistant Gram-Negative Infection
proposals.motivation: Colistin is a last-line antibiotic for multidrug-resistant Gram-negative infections. The global spread of colistin resistance, mediated by genes like mcr-1, represents a critical threat to public health. Identifying factors associated with a very low risk of such resistance could inform targeted stewardship and infection prevention strategies to preserve this vital therapeutic agent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distinct profile of patient-level clinical factors, antimicrobial exposure history, and specific infection prevention and control (IPC) practices within healthcare settings is associated with a significantly lower risk of developing colistin-resistant infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multicenter, matched case-control study. Cases will be patients with colistin-susceptible Gram-negative infections, matched 1:2 with controls (colistin-resistant infections) by pathogen, infection site, and hospital ward. Using multivariable conditional logistic regression, we will model factors associated with susceptibility. Key variables will include prior antibiotic exposures (especially colistin, carbapenems), healthcare exposure history, and facility-level IPC metrics (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing compliance, environmental cleaning audits), building on Dr. McKinnell's prior work in MDRO decolonization.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond identifying risk factors for resistance to explicitly model factors protective against it, providing a novel framework for resistance prevention. It will generate actionable data for antimicrobial stewardship and IPC programs, particularly in high-risk settings, and contribute to decolonization science by applying its principles to environmental and care practice determinants of resistance.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on risk factors for resistance emergence, this study uniquely inverts the paradigm to model the 'absence of resistance.' It integrates patient-level clinical data with detailed, facility-level care practice variables—a methodological combination not commonly applied to colistin resistance.

proposals.title: Decolonization and Beyond: Site-Specific Risk Models for Healthcare-Associated MRSA Infections in the Post-COVID Era
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted healthcare-associated infection (HAI) epidemiology and decolonization practices, potentially altering patient risk profiles. Generalized risk models for MRSA infection may be insufficient, as risk factors for respiratory (e.g., post-COVID lung injury), urinary (e.g., catheter use), and bloodstream infections (e.g., prior S. aureus colonization) likely differ. Developing infection site-specific models is crucial for targeted prevention and stewardship, especially in resource-limited settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, clinically significant sets of demographic, clinical, and microbiological risk factors predict incident MRSA respiratory tract infection (RTI), urinary tract infection (UTI), and bloodstream infection (BSI) in hospitalized adults, and that these site-specific models will outperform a single generic model.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study of adults hospitalized from 2020-2025. Using electronic health record data, we will define three separate cohorts based on culture-confirmed MRSA RTI, UTI, or BSI. For each cohort, we will use multivariable logistic regression with LASSO penalty for variable selection to build parsimonious risk models. Predictors will include pre-infection variables: prior MRSA colonization (from surveillance as in McKinnell's nursing home work), COVID-19 history, comorbidities, device use, and prior antibiotic exposure (including dalbavancin). Model performance will be assessed via area under the ROC curve (AUC) and compared to a pooled model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide clinicians with actionable, site-specific risk scores to guide empirical therapy and infection control precautions. It will inform the design of targeted decolonization strategies beyond universal bathing, potentially focusing on high-risk patients for specific infection types. The models will also quantify the evolving legacy of COVID-19 on HAI risk.
proposals.novelty: While risk models for MRSA bacteremia exist, a direct, comparative development of separate models for key infection sites using post-pandemic data is novel. Integrating decolonization history, detailed antibiotic exposure (including long-acting agents like dalbavancin), and COVID-19 status into these comparative models represents a significant advance over prior single-site or generic studies.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Outcomes of MRSA Colonization in Nursing Home Residents: A Prospective Cohort Study of Clinical Infection and Horizontal Transmission
proposals.motivation: Nursing homes are high-risk reservoirs for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like MRSA, where colonization is endemic. However, the precise risk that colonization poses to the colonized individual for subsequent clinical infection, and to their uncolonized roommates for transmission, remains poorly quantified in this setting. This knowledge gap hinders the development of targeted, evidence-based infection prevention strategies that balance efficacy with resident quality of life and decolonization burdens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MRSA colonization in nursing home residents is associated with a significantly increased risk of subsequent MRSA infection, and that colonized residents serve as a reservoir for transmission to their non-colonized roommates, with transmission risk modified by shared room occupancy time and functional status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month prospective cohort study in 5-10 nursing homes. All consenting residents will undergo baseline and quarterly nasal and inguinal swabbing for MRSA culture and molecular typing (e.g., spa typing). Colonized residents and their roommates will form the primary cohort. We will track clinical outcomes via standardized chart review for MRSA infections (e.g., skin/soft tissue, urinary, bloodstream). Transmission events will be defined by new, genetically concordant MRSA colonization in a previously negative roommate. We will use Cox proportional hazards models to analyze time-to-infection and time-to-transmission, adjusting for covariates like age, comorbidities, functional dependence, and recent antibiotic use.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, longitudinal estimates of the attributable risk of MRSA colonization for infection in a nursing home population, a critical metric for cost-benefit analyses of decolonization. It will directly quantify the risk of roommate-to-roommate transmission, informing contact isolation and roommate assignment policies. The findings will contribute a nuanced evidence base for decolonization strategies that are participant-centered and context-specific.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has assessed MDRO colonization prevalence or the impact of decolonization interventions, this study uniquely combines longitudinal molecular epidemiology with detailed clinical tracking to directly link an individual's colonization status to their own infection risk and their role in transmission within a shared living environment, moving beyond association to direct causal inference of transmission pathways.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Routine Multidrug-Resistant Organism (MDRO) Screening and Decolonization in Nursing Homes: A Decision-Analytic Model
proposals.motivation: Nursing homes (NHs) are high-risk reservoirs for MDROs like MRSA, leading to severe infections, hospital transfers, and significant healthcare costs. While decolonization strategies (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing) show promise, the economic value of routine screening to target these interventions remains unclear, especially in resource-limited NH settings. Determining cost-effectiveness is critical for informing infection prevention policy and resource allocation in post-acute care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strategy of routine MDRO screening followed by targeted decolonization for carriers will be cost-effective compared to universal decolonization or standard care (no screening) from a healthcare payer perspective.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-transition microsimulation model to project costs and health outcomes (e.g., MDRO infections, quality-adjusted life-years) for a hypothetical NH cohort over a one-year horizon. Model parameters (e.g., screening test characteristics, colonization prevalence, decolonization efficacy, infection risk, costs) will be derived from published literature (pre-2026), including McKinnell's prior work. We will perform deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses to identify key drivers of cost-effectiveness and assess uncertainty. The primary outcome will be the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) in dollars per QALY gained.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive economic model to guide NH infection control programs on the value of MDRO screening. It will quantify the trade-offs between different prevention strategies, offering evidence-based recommendations for administrators and policymakers. The findings will directly address a critical gap in translating clinical research on decolonization into practical, sustainable implementation.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have evaluated clinical outcomes of decolonization, this analysis uniquely integrates screening logistics, costs, and heterogeneous colonization risk into a formal economic model tailored for the NH environment. It moves beyond asking 'does it work?' to answer 'is it worth it?' for a specific, high-impact prevention strategy.

proposals.title: Clinical Outcomes of Severe Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus Endovascular Infections Treated with Bacteriostatic vs. Bactericidal Regimens: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) endovascular infections, such as endocarditis and bacteremia, are associated with high mortality and are notoriously difficult to treat. The standard of care, based on historical paradigms for other pathogens, prioritizes bactericidal therapy (e.g., daptomycin, linezolid combinations). However, the necessity of bactericidal activity for VRE specifically is an untested assumption, and potent bacteriostatic agents like oritavancin and telavancin are often relegated. Challenging this dogma is crucial for expanding therapeutic options and potentially improving outcomes through regimens with better pharmacokinetic or safety profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: For the treatment of severe VRE endovascular infections, therapeutic regimens classified as bacteriostatic in vitro will result in clinical outcomes (e.g., 30-day mortality, microbiologic cure) that are non-inferior to those achieved with regimens classified as bactericidal.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients (2015-2025) with VRE bacteremia and/or infective endocarditis. Patients will be stratified into two cohorts based on definitive antibiotic therapy: 'bactericidal' (e.g., high-dose daptomycin ± beta-lactams) or 'bacteriostatic' (e.g., linezolid monotherapy, oritavancin). The primary outcome is a composite of 30-day all-cause mortality and treatment failure. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders including severity of illness (APACHE II), source control, and comorbidities, drawing on Dr. McKinnell's expertise in analyzing clinical outcomes and colonization risk from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, comparative effectiveness data directly testing the bactericidal necessity paradigm for VRE. It will inform clinical guidelines by either validating current standards or identifying effective bacteriostatic alternatives, potentially expanding the antimicrobial arsenal. The findings will also contribute to the broader decolonization discourse by clarifying the role of antimicrobial activity type in managing resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: This directly inverts a core tenet of infectious disease therapy—the requirement for bactericidal activity in endovascular infections—for a specific, high-priority pathogen where it has never been rigorously evaluated. It leverages modern comparative effectiveness methods to challenge a historical assumption derived from experience with other Gram-positive cocci.

proposals.title: A Multicenter, Retrospective Cohort Study Assessing Clinical Outcomes of Linezolid Monotherapy for Deep-Seated Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus faecium Infections
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE), particularly E. faecium, is a leading cause of healthcare-associated infections with rising incidence. Deep-seated infections like endocarditis and endovascular infections present a major therapeutic challenge due to limited bactericidal options, high mortality, and the bacteriostatic nature of linezolid, a commonly used agent. Pre-2026 evidence for linezolid's efficacy in these severe, deep-seated VRE infections is limited to small case series and is confounded by combination therapy, creating significant uncertainty for clinicians.
proposals.hypothesis: Linezolid monotherapy is associated with non-inferior rates of clinical cure and mortality compared to combination antibiotic regimens for the treatment of deep-seated VRE faecium infections, despite its bacteriostatic mechanism.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multicenter cohort study of adult patients (2015-2025) with deep-seated VRE faecium infections (endocarditis, septic thrombophlebitis, infected aortic grafts). Using electronic health record data, we will define two cohorts: those treated with linezolid monotherapy and those with combination therapy (e.g., daptomycin-based). The primary outcome will be a composite of 90-day all-cause mortality and clinical failure. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders including severity of illness (APACHE-II), source control, and comorbidities. A time-to-event analysis for mortality will also be performed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the largest comparative effectiveness data to date on linezolid for severe VRE infections, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and treatment guidelines. It will clarify the real-world role of a bacteriostatic agent in endovascular infections, addressing a critical evidence gap. The findings will also contribute to understanding the clinical implications of bacteriostatic vs. bactericidal therapy in high-inoculum infections.
proposals.novelty: While linezolid is used for VRE, its evaluation specifically in deep-seated, endovascular infections—where bactericidal therapy is traditionally preferred—is underexplored. This study innovatively applies robust epidemiologic methods (propensity scoring) to a clinically urgent, high-mortality scenario where randomized trials are scarce, leveraging real-world data to generate practice-guiding evidence.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Dalbavancin versus Standard-of-Care for S. aureus Bacteremia in High-Risk, Decolonization-Eligible Populations: A Real-World Evidence Study Using National Registry Data
proposals.motivation: While RCTs like DOTS establish dalbavancin's efficacy, its comparative effectiveness in real-world, high-risk populations—particularly those eligible for MRSA decolonization (e.g., nursing home residents, post-COVID-19 patients with lingering sequelae)—remains unclear. These patients are often excluded from trials, yet they face high rates of recurrence and mortality, creating an evidence gap for clinicians managing complex S. aureus infections.
proposals.hypothesis: Among patients with S. aureus bacteremia who are candidates for MRSA decolonization, treatment with dalbavancin will be associated with a lower risk of 90-day bacteremia recurrence and all-cause hospitalization compared to standard intravenous antibiotic regimens, after rigorous adjustment for confounding.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked data from the Veterans Health Administration and CMS, identifying adults with S. aureus bacteremia treated with dalbavancin or standard care (e.g., vancomycin, daptomycin). High-risk, decolonization-eligible participants will be defined using diagnosis codes for prior MRSA colonization, nursing home residence, or recent severe COVID-19 hospitalization. We will use propensity score matching and inverse probability of treatment weighting to control for confounding. The primary outcome is a composite of bacteremia recurrence or death within 90 days, analyzed using Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial real-world evidence on the effectiveness of a long-acting antibiotic in a vulnerable, understudied population, directly informing post-discharge treatment strategies. It will also demonstrate a methodological framework for using registry data to answer comparative effectiveness questions in infectious diseases where pragmatic RCTs are challenging.
proposals.novelty: This integrates Dr. McKinnell's expertise in decolonization, MRSA, and COVID-19 outcomes to frame a novel pharmacoepidemiological question, moving beyond trial efficacy to assess effectiveness in a complex, real-world cohort where decolonization is a concurrent clinical consideration.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Modeling to Optimize Daptomycin and Linezolid Dosing for Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus Bloodstream Infections in Critically Ill Patients: A Population PK/PD and Clinical Outcomes Study
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) bloodstream infections (BSI) are a significant cause of mortality, particularly in critically ill patients with complex physiology that alters antibiotic pharmacokinetics. Standard dosing regimens for first-line agents like daptomycin and linezolid, derived from studies in less severe illness, may lead to suboptimal drug exposure and treatment failure in this vulnerable population. This research addresses a critical gap in precision dosing for a serious, multidrug-resistant infection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) model, incorporating patient-specific covariates (e.g., renal function, fluid status, serum albumin), will identify that a significant proportion of critically ill patients with VRE-BSI fail to achieve PK/PD targets associated with microbiological cure with standard antibiotic dosing.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of critically ill adults with VRE-BSI treated with daptomycin or linezolid. Serial blood samples will be collected to measure antibiotic concentrations. Data will be analyzed using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM v7.5) to build population PK models. Covariate analysis will identify clinical factors (e.g., augmented renal clearance, obesity) driving PK variability. Monte Carlo simulations (n=10,000) will assess the probability of target attainment (PTA) for various dosing regimens against VRE MIC distributions, using established PK/PD indices (fAUC/MIC for linezolid, fCmax/MIC for daptomycin).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, covariate-driven PK models for daptomycin and linezolid specifically in critically ill VRE-BSI patients. It will generate evidence-based, optimized dosing algorithms tailored to patient physiology to maximize PK/PD target attainment. The findings will directly inform clinical practice and future interventional trials aimed at improving outcomes for this hard-to-treat infection.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD modeling is standard in antibiotic development, its application to optimize dosing for established antibiotics in the specific, high-risk context of VRE-BSI in critical illness is novel. This work integrates detailed PK sampling, advanced modeling, and clinical outcomes to move beyond one-size-fits-all dosing, aligning with Dr. McKinnell's expertise in colonization, infection risk, and clinical outcomes research in resistant pathogens.

proposals.title: Host-Directed Pathogenesis in Vancomycin-Treated S. aureus Bacteremia: A Prospective Cohort Study of Immune Dysregulation and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Despite appropriate vancomycin dosing, treatment failure in S. aureus bacteremia (SAB) remains common, often attributed to MIC creep or heteroresistance. However, pre-2026 research increasingly recognizes that bacterial virulence factors, like pore-forming toxins, cause significant host cell damage, potentially driving poor outcomes independent of resistance. This host-pathogen interaction is a critical, underexplored dimension of therapeutic failure, especially in vulnerable populations with high SAB burdens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in patients with vancomycin-treated SAB, high levels of host cell damage biomarkers, indicative of toxin-mediated pathogenesis, will be independently associated with worse clinical outcomes (e.g., persistent bacteremia, recurrence, mortality), even after controlling for vancomycin MIC and other traditional risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of adults hospitalized with MRSA or MSSA bacteremia. Serial blood samples will be collected to measure host biomarkers of cell damage (e.g., ADAM10 activity, lactate dehydrogenase) and immune dysregulation (e.g., IL-1β, IL-6). Clinical data on vancomycin pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics, comorbidities, and decolonization history will be collected. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models to assess the independent association of biomarker trajectories with a composite clinical failure outcome, adjusting for MIC, source control, and APACHE II score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct clinical evidence that host-directed pathogenesis is a key driver of vancomycin failure, shifting the paradigm beyond MIC-centric views. It will identify specific, measurable biomarkers that could stratify patient risk and future trials of adjuvant host-directed therapies (e.g., toxin-neutralizing antibodies) in SAB.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies associate virulence genes with outcomes, this proposal directly links real-time, quantitative measures of host cell damage during infection to clinical failure in a treated cohort, integrating host response data with detailed pharmacokinetic and microbiological parameters.

proposals.title: Association of In Vitro Endothelial Cell Damage with Clinical Severity and Outcomes in Patients with Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus Endocarditis
proposals.motivation: MRSA endocarditis is a severe infection with high mortality and morbidity, partly due to vascular damage and metastatic complications. Pre-2026 research has established that S. aureus strains vary in their capacity to damage endothelial cells (ECs) in vitro, but the clinical relevance of this phenotypic variation is unknown. Validating EC damage as a predictive biomarker could stratify patient risk and guide more aggressive or targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: The degree of endothelial cell damage caused by a patient's infecting MRSA isolate in vitro is positively associated with clinical disease severity, metastatic complication rates, and all-cause mortality in human endocarditis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of participants with definite MRSA endocarditis per Modified Duke Criteria. Clinical isolates will be collected and used in a standardized in vitro assay quantifying lactate dehydrogenase release from human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) after co-culture. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models to test the association between the quantitative EC damage index (primary exposure) and time-to-death/composite clinical failure (primary outcome), adjusting for known confounders like age, comorbidities, and vancomycin MIC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical validation of a functional bacterial virulence assay as a prognostic tool in MRSA endocarditis. It will establish a framework for integrating pathogen-specific phenotypes into risk prediction models, moving beyond genotypic markers. The findings could inform future trials of adjunctive therapies aimed at mitigating endothelial damage in high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by McKinnell and others has linked MRSA colonization to infection risk and studied decolonization, directly translating a specific in vitro virulence phenotype to patient-level outcomes in a complex infection like endocarditis is novel. This bridges mechanistic microbiology and clinical epidemiology in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Endothelial Cell Damage as a Predictor of Clinical Severity in Community-Onset MRSA Sepsis: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. McKinnell's work on COVID-19 pneumonia and MRSA bacteremia, highlights the critical role of host endothelial cell (EC) injury in driving poor outcomes in severe infections. While EC damage is a known feature of severe COVID-19 and S. aureus bloodstream infections, its specific correlation with disease severity across the spectrum of community-onset MRSA sepsis remains poorly characterized. Defining this relationship is crucial for developing host-directed biomarkers to risk-stratify patients and guide targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the magnitude of circulating endothelial cell damage biomarkers at presentation correlates directly with the clinical severity and 30-day outcomes of patients with community-onset MRSA sepsis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of adult participants hospitalized with culture-confirmed MRSA sepsis. Plasma collected at enrollment will be analyzed for established EC damage biomarkers (syndecan-1, soluble thrombomodulin, and endothelial-derived extracellular vesicles) via multiplex ELISA and nanoparticle tracking analysis. Clinical severity will be quantified using sequential SOFA scores, need for ICU admission/vasopressors, and 30-day mortality. Multivariable regression, adjusting for associated risk factors like age and comorbidities, will assess the correlation between biomarker levels and outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a direct, quantitative link between EC injury and MRSA sepsis severity, moving beyond bacteremia to a broader sepsis population. It will validate specific EC damage biomarkers as potential prognostic tools for clinical risk stratification. The findings could inform future trials of endothelial-protective adjunctive therapies in severe staphylococcal disease.
proposals.novelty: This work extends the EC damage paradigm from COVID-19 and bacteremia trials to the understudied context of community-onset MRSA sepsis, integrating decolonization research principles by considering host vulnerability. It applies a multi-biomarker panel, rather than a single marker, to define a composite EC damage phenotype predictive of clinical course.

proposals.title: Efficacy of a Post-Discharge Pulse Decolonization Protocol versus Standard 6-Month Regimen for Preventing MRSA Infection and Rehospitalization: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Decolonization with chlorhexidine and nasal antiseptics is a key strategy to prevent Staphylococcus aureus infections in high-risk patients, but standard protocols often recommend burdensome 6-month regimens. These lengthy courses may promote antimicrobial resistance, reduce patient adherence, and increase healthcare costs. A precision approach targeting the period of highest vulnerability could maintain efficacy while mitigating these drawbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted 14-day decolonization 'pulse' initiated immediately after hospital discharge will be non-inferior to a standard 6-month decolonization regimen in preventing MRSA clinical infection and infection-related rehospitalization over a 12-month follow-up period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, open-label, randomized non-inferiority trial. Adults colonized with MRSA who are being discharged from hospital to home will be randomized 1:1 to either a 14-day post-discharge pulse (chlorhexidine bathing and nasal iodophor) or a standard 180-day regimen. The primary outcome is a composite of MRSA infection or infection-related rehospitalization within 12 months. Secondary outcomes include time to first event, all-cause mortality, patient adherence, and cost-effectiveness. A sample size of 450 participants per arm will provide 80% power to detect non-inferiority with a margin of 5%.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for a patient-centered, stewardship-focused decolonization strategy that reduces treatment burden. It will directly inform clinical guidelines on the necessary duration of decolonization for secondary prevention. The findings could shift the paradigm from prolonged suppression to targeted, risk-period intervention, aligning with principles of antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the foundational assumption that decolonization requires long-term suppression, translating the 'pulse therapy' concept from agricultural antibiotic stewardship to human clinical practice. The intervention is precisely timed to the documented high-risk transition period immediately post-discharge, a novel application of risk-stratified timing.

proposals.title: Network-Informed Precision Decolonization: An Inverse Modeling Approach to Target Short-Course Regimens for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) in Post-Acute Care Transitions
proposals.motivation: The spread of multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like CRE across healthcare networks is a critical public health threat. Standard decolonization protocols are often prolonged, resource-intensive, and applied indiscriminately, leading to poor adherence and antimicrobial resistance pressure. A precision strategy is needed to efficiently disrupt transmission by targeting interventions at key network nodes, especially among patients discharging to high-risk settings like nursing homes.
proposals.hypothesis: Using inverse transmission modeling to identify patients most likely to be sources or conduits of ongoing CRE transmission will allow a targeted, short-course decolonization intervention at hospital discharge to reduce subsequent CRE prevalence in the network as effectively as a standard, longer-duration protocol applied to all colonized patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a stepped-wedge cluster-randomized trial across a regional hospital and its connected nursing homes. Using pre-intervention admission and CRE surveillance data, we will fit an inverse transmission model (e.g., a Bayesian source attribution or network inference model) to reconstruct probable transmission pathways. Discharged patients will be stratified: the intervention arm receives a 5-day post-discharge decolonization (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing plus nasal iodophor) only if the model identifies them as high-risk network nodes. The primary outcome is the change in unit-level CRE prevalence in nursing homes over 6 months, compared to a control phase using standard practice.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for integrating computational epidemiology into real-time infection prevention. It will generate evidence for a resource-efficient, patient-centered decolonization strategy that minimizes antimicrobial exposure while targeting transmission dynamics. The findings could redefine how healthcare networks use data to deploy precision interventions against MDROs.
proposals.novelty: While inverse modeling has been used for outbreak reconstruction, its application to prospectively guide a clinical intervention is novel. The fusion of network inference with a pragmatic, short-course decolonization trial moves beyond descriptive epidemiology to active, model-driven prevention.

proposals.title: Ecological Niche Displacement Following Targeted MRSA Decolonization: A Prospective Cohort Study to Predict Emergence of Competing Pathogens
proposals.motivation: Targeted MRSA decolonization is a cornerstone infection prevention strategy, but its long-term ecological consequences are poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. McKinnell's work in nursing homes, show decolonization reduces MRSA but may increase other multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs), suggesting an unintended niche-filling event. This research addresses a critical gap in decolonization science by moving from observing replacement to predicting it, which is essential for mitigating risk in vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ecological niche occupied by MRSA in colonized patients can be quantitatively defined, and that upon MRSA decolonization, the vacated niche will be predictably filled by other pathogens whose intrinsic niche requirements most closely match the newly available host environment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 500 MRSA-colonized adults undergoing decolonization with chlorhexidine and nasal iodophor. We will define the MRSA niche using serial metagenomic sequencing (pre- and post-decolonization) to map microbial community structure, host transcriptomic profiling of nasal and skin immune markers, and patient clinical metadata. Niche overlap and competitive release will be modeled using Lotka-Volterra competition equations and null model analysis from community ecology, with machine learning (Random Forest) used to integrate multi-omic and clinical data to predict which specific pathogen (e.g., VRE, P. aeruginosa, C. auris) emerges.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first predictive framework for pathogen replacement following decolonization, identifying high-risk patients for targeted surveillance. It will generate a novel, ecologically grounded dataset linking host immunity, microbiome, and pathogen dynamics, directly informing more precise and safer decolonization protocols.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in explicitly applying formal ecological niche theory and competition models, standard in macroecology, to the clinical problem of pathogen decolonization, moving beyond descriptive association to mechanistic prediction.

proposals.title: Behavioral Adherence vs. Chemical Decolonization: A Cluster-Randomized Trial on Infection Prevention and Resistance Selection in High-Risk Nursing Home Residents
proposals.motivation: Routine chlorhexidine bathing and nasal decolonization are widely implemented to prevent healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) like MRSA, but they exert selective pressure for resistance genes (e.g., qac, mupA). This creates a critical tension between immediate patient safety and long-term antimicrobial stewardship. A decolonization-informed approach must evaluate whether intensive, sustained behavioral interventions can achieve comparable infection prevention without driving resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: A high-intensity, technology-augmented hygiene education program will be non-inferior to standard chemical decolonization in preventing target infections, while resulting in a significantly lower prevalence and genomic carriage of antimicrobial resistance genes and multi-drug resistant organisms over time.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month, cluster-randomized trial in 30 nursing homes. Clusters will be randomized to: 1) Standard Decolonization (chlorhexidine bathing, nasal iodophor/mupirocin), or 2) Intensive Education (a structured, multi-modal program using tablet-based tutorials, gamified adherence tracking, and environmental sensor feedback). Primary outcomes are time to first target infection (non-inferiority analysis) and quarterly genomic surveillance of nasal/pooled skin swabs via whole-genome sequencing to quantify resistance gene abundance and MDRO strain dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, longitudinal comparison of infection prevention efficacy and resistance selection pressure between behavioral and chemical strategies. It will generate actionable evidence for infection prevention protocols that balance patient safety with stewardship. The findings will inform policy on sustainable decolonization practices in long-term care settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied education or decolonization in isolation, this trial directly links the efficacy of a structured behavioral intervention to genomic markers of resistance, a novel fusion of implementation science and molecular epidemiology to address a core dilemma in infection prevention.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Drivers of Surgical Site Infection Prevention: A Randomized Biomarker and Microbiome Trial of Targeted vs. Broad-Spectrum Decolonization
proposals.motivation: Pre-operative decolonization reduces surgical site infections (SSIs), but its benefits are often attributed to a monolithic 'general antiseptic effect.' This obscures whether the primary mechanism is pathogen-specific suppression (e.g., MRSA), reduction of total bacterial burden, or modulation of the host inflammatory state. Disentangling these pathways is critical for decolonization science and for developing optimized, ecologically-informed regimens that avoid unintended consequences like antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the clinical benefit of pre-operative decolonization is driven by distinct, measurable mechanisms: targeted regimens primarily reduce pathogen transmission, while broad-spectrum antiseptics reduce total bacterial burden and dampen local pro-inflammatory signaling, with these mechanistic differences explaining variations in SSI outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-arm, parallel-group randomized controlled trial in adults undergoing major surgery. Participants will be randomized to: 1) targeted MRSA decolonization (mupirocin ointment), 2) broad-spectrum antiseptic (2% chlorhexidine gluconate cloths), or 3) standard non-antimicrobial soap (control). Serial quantitative cultures from nares and incision site, metagenomic sequencing of the surgical site microbiome, and plasma/skin cytokine levels (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α) will be collected pre- and post-intervention. The primary clinical outcome is a composite of SSIs within 30 days. Core analysis will use multivariable regression and mediation analysis to test causal pathways linking intervention arm to biomarker/microbiome shifts and, subsequently, to SSI risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from a black-box assessment of decolonization efficacy to a mechanistic understanding of its action. It will provide a biomarker-driven framework for evaluating antiseptic interventions and generate high-resolution data on how different strategies alter the surgical site microbiome and host response, informing more precise and personalized prevention guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior trials (including McKinnell's nursing home work) compare decolonization to control, this study is the first to directly contrast targeted and broad-spectrum mechanisms within a single trial and use integrated biomarker and metagenomic data to formally test causal pathways of benefit, moving beyond correlative associations.

proposals.title: Decoupling Antiseptic Effect from Pathogen-Specific Decolonization: A Re-analysis of MRSA Prevention Trial Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Universal decolonization with chlorhexidine gluconate (CHG) bathing and nasal antiseptics is increasingly adopted to prevent healthcare-associated infections (HAIs), with MRSA reduction often cited as the mechanism. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests CHG has broad-spectrum activity. This creates a critical knowledge gap: are observed clinical benefits (e.g., reduced bloodstream infections) truly mediated by MRSA decolonization, or are they a consequence of a general reduction in the overall bacterial burden?
proposals.hypothesis: The clinical benefit (reduction in all-cause bloodstream infections) observed in MRSA decolonization trials is not specifically mediated by a reduction in MRSA colonization or infection, but is instead a non-specific effect attributable to the general antiseptic reduction of the total skin and nasal microbial burden.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary, patient-level analysis of data from a large, pre-2026 cluster-randomized trial of universal CHG and nasal iodophor decolonization in nursing homes (e.g., akin to the referenced 'Project PROTECT' or similar). Using causal mediation analysis, we will model 'reduction in MRSA colonization' as the putative mediator and 'all-cause bloodstream infection' as the outcome. The analysis will quantify the proportion of the total treatment effect on outcomes that is explained by the MRSA-specific pathway versus the direct (non-mediated) effect of the antiseptic regimen. Sensitivity analyses will adjust for baseline colonization status and competing risks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic understanding of how universal decolonization works, challenging the pathogen-specific narrative. It will inform more precise clinical guidelines and health economic models by clarifying the true active component of the intervention. The findings could also support the development of next-generation, broad-spectrum antiseptic strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates decolonization with reduced infection rates, this study directly tests the causal pathway using advanced mediation models on trial data, explicitly decoupling the MRSA-specific effect from the general antiseptic effect, a distinction not made in previous analyses.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Randomized Trial of Algorithm-Guided, Genotype-Informed Decolonization to Reduce Staphylococcus aureus Infection in High-Risk Inpatients
proposals.motivation: Universal decolonization protocols, while effective, are a blunt instrument. They ignore individual colonization profiles, potentially driving resistance in non-target organisms and failing against strains with biocide resistance genes (e.g., qacA/B, mupA). The COVID-19 pandemic has heightened the acuity of hospitalized patients, increasing the risk of subsequent nosocomial infections like MRSA bacteremia, which carries significant mortality. A precision approach is needed to optimize efficacy and stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive decolonization regimen, personalized via a clinical algorithm incorporating rapid genomic detection of S. aureus strain, burden, and resistance markers, will yield superior clinical effectiveness (reduced S. aureus infections) and ecological impact (less disruption to the microbiome and resistance selection) compared to a standard 5-day chlorhexidine/mupirocin protocol.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, assessor-blinded RCT in adults admitted with prior MRSA history or high-risk comorbidities. The intervention arm uses a pre-treatment nasal swab analyzed by rapid multiplex PCR (e.g., BioFire FilmArray) for S. aureus/MRSA, quantification, and mupA/qacA/B genes. Results feed into a rule-based algorithm (developed via Delphi consensus) that recommends: 1) no decolonization (low burden, no target), 2) chlorhexidine only (mupA+), 3) mupirocin only (qacA/B+), or 4) both agents, with duration (3-7 days) adjusted by burden. The control arm receives standard protocol. Primary outcome is 90-day S. aureus clinical infection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-level evidence for a precision decolonization strategy in acute care.
