It will yield a validated, implementable clinical decision support algorithm and generate critical data on the real-world prevalence and impact of biocide resistance genes on decolonization failure, informing future guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing in nursing homes) applies decolonization universally, this study dynamically personalizes both the agent choice and duration based on a rapid molecular profile, moving from a static infection prevention policy to an adaptive, patient-specific intervention.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Trial to Optimize the Duration and Frequency of Chlorhexidine and Mupirocin Decolonization for MRSA Prevention in High-Risk Outpatients
proposals.motivation: Decolonization with chlorhexidine gluconate (CHG) bathing and nasal mupirocin is a key strategy to prevent Staphylococcus aureus infections, including MRSA, in high-risk populations. However, standard protocols derived from inpatient settings (e.g., daily application for 5-14 days) present significant adherence burdens and potential for skin irritation in community-dwelling patients with recurrent infections or indwelling devices, potentially undermining long-term efficacy. An evidence-based, patient-centered schedule that balances microbiological efficacy with practical feasibility is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A shorter, intermittent decolonization regimen (e.g., twice-weekly CHG baths with bi-weekly mupirocin) will demonstrate non-inferiority in reducing S. aureus clinical infection rates compared to a standard daily regimen over a 6-month period, while significantly improving participant-reported adherence and skin tolerance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, cluster-randomized, non-inferiority trial in outpatient dialysis units and outpatient parenteral antimicrobial therapy (OPAT) clinics. Clusters (clinical sites) will be randomized to implement one of three decolonization schedules for MRSA-colonized participants: 1) Standard (daily CHG/mupirocin for 5 days, monthly), 2) Short-course (daily for 2 days, bi-weekly), or 3) Pulse (twice-weekly CHG, mupirocin once weekly). The primary outcome is time to first symptomatic S. aureus infection (bacteremia, skin/soft tissue) over 6 months. Secondary outcomes include quantitative S. aureus colonization burden (via serial nares/axilla cultures), adherence measured by electronic bottle weights and self-report, and dermatologic adverse events. Analysis will use mixed-effects Cox proportional hazards and generalized estimating equation models, adjusting for cluster and individual-level covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from a pragmatic trial to guide the operationalization of decolonization in ambulatory care, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will generate critical data on the real-world adherence-efficacy trade-off, potentially defining a more sustainable and user-friendly standard of care for long-term infection prevention in vulnerable populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has established *that* decolonization works, this study uniquely focuses on *how* to best implement it in real-world, high-risk outpatient settings by systematically testing schedule optimization—a key gap identified in implementation science. It moves beyond efficacy to explicitly prioritize adherence and tolerability as co-primary determinants of long-term success.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of an Ecological Maintenance Protocol for Long-Term Decolonization of Multidrug-Resistant Organisms in High-Risk Patients Post-Hospital Discharge
proposals.motivation: Patients colonized with multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like VRE and resistant Gram-negatives act as reservoirs for transmission and subsequent infection. Current decolonization strategies, often limited to the inpatient setting or a single post-discharge intervention, frequently fail due to re-colonization from the home environment. This creates a critical gap in infection prevention, analogous to the failure of one-time containment efforts against invasive species without sustained habitat management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a post-discharge, ecologically-informed maintenance protocol—combining periodic molecular surveillance, on-demand targeted decolonization, and concurrent environmental decontamination—will be superior to a single post-discharge decolonization regimen in achieving sustained (6-month) MDRO decolonization and reducing household environmental contamination.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, randomized controlled trial in adults discharged from hospital with known VRE or resistant Gram-negative colonization. The intervention arm will receive: 1) initial decolonization (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing), 2) bi-weekly self-collected rectal/inguinal swabs returned by mail for PCR-based surveillance, 3) a short, prescribed decolonization course triggered by any positive result, and 4) EPA-registered disinfectants and standardized cleaning instructions for high-touch surfaces. The control arm will receive initial decolonization only. The primary outcome is MDRO-free colonization status at 6 months, assessed by culture. Secondary outcomes include time to re-colonization, household environmental burden, and healthcare re-utilization for infection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical evidence for a dynamic, surveillance-driven decolonization strategy in the community, moving beyond static protocols. It will generate practical data on the feasibility and efficacy of integrating patient self-sampling with environmental interventions to disrupt the MDRO transmission cycle beyond hospital walls, directly informing post-discharge infection prevention guidelines.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual translation of invasive species eradication principles—specifically sustained surveillance and habitat modification—to clinical decolonization, and in its operationalization of a feedback loop where molecular diagnostics trigger personalized, on-demand decolonization in the home setting.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Post-Discharge Decolonization to Reduce Subsequent Clinical Infection and Rehospitalization in Carriers of Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococci (VRE) or Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE)
proposals.motivation: Decolonization strategies for MRSA have shown promise in reducing post-discharge infections, but similar evidence is lacking for other high-priority multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like VRE and resistant Gram-negative bacteria. These pathogens are a major cause of healthcare-associated infections and contribute to poor long-term outcomes, particularly in vulnerable patients transitioning from hospital to community settings. A targeted intervention in this high-risk population is needed to break the cycle of colonization, recurrent infection, and rehospitalization.
proposals.hypothesis: A post-discharge decolonization protocol, adapted from successful MRSA models, will reduce the composite endpoint of clinical infection or rehospitalization due to the target MDRO within 90 days of discharge among known carriers of VRE or CRE.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, open-label, randomized controlled trial. Adult patients identified as VRE or CRE carriers via clinical culture or active surveillance upon hospital admission will be randomized at discharge. The intervention arm will receive a 30-day supply of chlorhexidine gluconate (CHG) for daily bathing and a topical agent for nasal application (e.g., povidone-iodine, based on pre-2026 evidence for Gram-negative suppression), plus standardized education. The control arm will receive standard education only. The primary outcome is a composite of MDRO-specific clinical infection or rehospitalization for any cause within 90 days, assessed via electronic health record review and patient phone interviews. Analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with logistic regression, adjusting for key covariates like comorbidities and prior healthcare exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the efficacy of a post-discharge decolonization bundle targeting non-MRSA MDROs, directly informing infection prevention guidelines for high-risk carriers. It will generate critical data on the feasibility and impact of extending decolonization paradigms beyond Staphylococcus aureus, potentially reducing the long-term clinical burden and healthcare costs associated with VRE and CRE.
proposals.novelty: While decolonization research has focused heavily on MRSA and the inpatient setting, this proposal uniquely applies the post-discharge intervention model—a strategy of growing importance for MRSA—to the understudied population of VRE and CRE carriers, addressing a significant evidence gap in infection prevention.

proposals.title: Quantifying Community-Level MRSA Decolonization Impact via Wastewater-Based Epidemiology: A Longitudinal Surveillance Study
proposals.motivation: Decolonization interventions (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing, nasal iodophor) are increasingly deployed in nursing homes and other congregate settings to reduce MRSA burden, but their population-level efficacy beyond clinical settings is poorly understood. Pre-2026, wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) has proven transformative for tracking SARS-CoV-2 and polio, offering a non-invasive, aggregate measure of community infection. Applying this tool to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) surveillance, specifically MRSA, could provide a novel metric for public health impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the genomic load of MRSA in community wastewater will significantly decrease following the systematic implementation of a targeted decolonization protocol in participating nursing homes, and that these trends will correlate with reductions in clinical MRSA colonization rates among participants.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal study over 12 months, collecting weekly wastewater samples from the influent of a municipal treatment plant serving communities with participating nursing homes. Using quantitative PCR (qPCR), we will target the *mecA* gene and *S. aureus*-specific *nuc* gene to quantify MRSA load. Samples will be further analyzed via droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) for higher precision and multiplexed assays for common SCC*mec* types. Concurrently, we will collect monthly clinical colonization data (nasal and inguinal swabs) from consenting nursing home residents as part of the decolonization intervention. Time-series analysis will correlate wastewater MRSA signals with clinical data and intervention timelines.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish WBE as a practical tool for monitoring the community-wide impact of MRSA decolonization programs, moving beyond facility-specific audits. It will generate a novel, objective, and population-level dataset to inform public health strategies for AMR containment and evaluate the real-world effectiveness of infection prevention interventions.
proposals.novelty: While WBE for SARS-CoV-2 is established, its application to quantify the impact of a deliberate infection control intervention (decolonization) on a bacterial pathogen at the community scale is novel. This bridges the fields of clinical infection prevention, decolonization research, and environmental microbiology in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling and Simulation of Ceftazidime-Avibactam Dosing Regimens for Pathogens with Elevated MICs in Critically Ill Patients Receiving Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy
proposals.motivation: Ceftazidime-avibactam (CAZ-AVI) is a critical agent for carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales, but clinical failures are reported when the pathogen MIC is >8 µg/mL, a susceptibility breakpoint. Critically ill patients on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) have highly variable pharmacokinetics, risking subtherapeutic exposure against these higher-MIC pathogens, which can lead to treatment failure and drive further resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimized, intensified CAZ-AVI dosing regimens, informed by pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) targets, will achieve a high probability of target attainment (>90% fT>8xMIC) for pathogens with MICs of 16-32 µg/mL in a CRRT patient population.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational pharmacokinetic study in 30 adult ICU patients receiving CAZ-AVI while on CRRT. Serial plasma samples will be collected over a dosing interval. Population PK models for ceftazidime and avibactam will be developed using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM). Covariates (e.g., CRRT effluent rate, residual diuresis) will be tested. Monte Carlo simulations (n=10,000) will evaluate probability of target attainment for standard and intensified regimens (e.g., increased dose, prolonged infusion) against MICs from 8 to 32 µg/mL.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first model-based, CRRT-specific dosing recommendations for CAZ-AVI against high-MIC pathogens, directly addressing a precarious clinical scenario. It will offer a practical framework for precision dosing to improve outcomes in this vulnerable population and preserve the utility of a last-resort antibiotic.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe CAZ-AVI PK in CRRT, none have specifically modeled and simulated optimized dosing against the elevated MICs (16-32 µg/mL) associated with treatment failure, integrating real-world CRRT operational parameters into the dosing strategy.

proposals.title: A Multi-Center Registry Study of Pharmacokinetics, Pharmacodynamics, and Clinical Outcomes for Novel Anti-Staphylococcal Agents in Critically Ill Patients Receiving Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy
proposals.motivation: Critically ill patients on Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) are at high risk for severe infections, including MRSA bacteremia, yet optimal antibiotic dosing in this population remains poorly defined. Pre-2026, novel agents like dalbavancin and other long-acting lipoglycopeptides are being evaluated, but their PK/PD during CRRT is extrapolated from small, single-center studies, risking treatment failure or toxicity. This knowledge gap directly impacts clinical outcomes in a vulnerable, decolonization-resistant population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-center registry will identify significant inter-center variability in CRRT-related PK parameters for novel anti-staphylococcal agents, and that this variability is associated with modifiable CRRT operational factors and differential risks of clinical failure or recurrence.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective, observational registry across 8-10 academic medical centers with high-volume CRRT programs. The registry will collect detailed, timed PK samples, CRRT operational parameters (mode, effluent rate, filter type), and clinical outcomes for patients receiving novel antibiotics (e.g., dalbavancin, oritavancin, novel beta-lactams) for S. aureus infections. Population PK modeling (using NONMEM) will be employed to characterize drug clearance and volume of distribution. Multivariable logistic regression will assess the association between PK/PD target attainment (e.g., fAUC/MIC) and the primary outcome of 30-day clinical failure.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the largest real-world PK/PD dataset for novel antibiotics in CRRT, enabling data-driven, patient-specific dosing recommendations. It will directly inform stewardship and improve the care of a high-risk population by linking CRRT practice to antibiotic exposure and hard clinical outcomes, moving beyond surrogate markers.
proposals.novelty: While prior PK studies in CRRT exist, this is the first to systematically link multi-center variability in CRRT implementation to antibiotic exposure and patient-centered outcomes for the newest anti-MRSA agents, creating a platform for rapid, post-marketing PK/PD surveillance.

proposals.title: Heterogeneous Transmission Dynamics of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) in Nursing Homes: A Genomic and Quantitative Burden Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Current infection prevention models for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) in long-term care facilities often assume uniform transmissibility across strains. This simplification may misrepresent the true risk landscape, particularly for CRE, a critical public health threat. Integrating genomic data and bacterial burden into transmission models is essential for developing targeted, resource-efficient decolonization and infection control strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CRE transmission potential in nursing home settings is heterogeneous, driven by specific genetic determinants (e.g., carbapenemase gene type, plasmid lineages) and modulated by the colonized host's bacterial burden.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study in 3-5 nursing homes, enrolling participants with known or newly identified CRE colonization. Serial peri-rectal swabs will quantify bacterial burden via quantitative PCR targeting the blaKPC, blaNDM, or blaOXA-48 genes. Whole-genome sequencing of isolates will identify strain types, resistance genes, and plasmid backbones. A modified susceptible-colonized transmission model will be implemented using a Bayesian framework (e.g., Stan), where the transmission rate parameter (β) is modeled as a function of genetic cluster and log-transformed bacterial burden.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven transmission model for CRE that moves beyond the uniform transmissibility assumption. It will identify high-risk strain clusters and colonization burdens, directly informing precision infection control. The findings will contribute to the decolonization research agenda by suggesting which patients and strains might benefit most from targeted interventions like chlorhexidine or nasal iodophor.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (including the PI's) has assessed MDRO colonization and environmental contamination, explicitly modeling transmission rates as a function of both real-time molecular epidemiology and quantitative bacterial burden in a nursing home setting is novel. This integrates clinical microbiology, genomics, and mathematical epidemiology in a way not previously applied to CRE in long-term care.

proposals.title: Modeling the Impact of Heterogeneous Patient Adherence on the Community-Level Effectiveness of Universal Decolonization at Hospital Discharge
proposals.motivation: Universal decolonization at discharge is a promising strategy to reduce the community reservoir of multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like MRSA, potentially interrupting the cycle of re-admission and transmission. However, its real-world effectiveness hinges on patient adherence to protocols like chlorhexidine bathing and nasal decolonization at home, which is known to be variable and suboptimal. Pre-2026, models of such interventions often assume perfect compliance, creating a significant evidence gap for predicting population-level impact and cost-effectiveness under realistic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating heterogeneous, real-world levels of patient adherence into transmission models will reveal a non-linear relationship between adherence and community MRSA prevalence reduction, and that the intervention's cost-effectiveness and resistance selection risk are highly sensitive to adherence rates below a critical threshold.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid agent-based and compartmental transmission model, parameterized with pre-2026 clinical data from studies on MRSA colonization, decolonization efficacy (e.g., chlorhexidine/iodophor), and discharge patterns. The model will simulate a closed population with hospital and community compartments. A key innovation is the agent-based modeling of discharged patient cohorts, where each 'agent' is assigned an adherence probability drawn from beta distributions informed by empirical post-discharge compliance studies. We will use Monte Carlo simulation and sensitivity analysis (e.g., using Latin Hypercube Sampling) to quantify outcomes across a wide range of adherence scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework to estimate the community-level effectiveness of universal decolonization under realistic adherence constraints. It will identify minimum adherence thresholds required for the strategy to be cost-effective compared to targeted approaches, and will generate evidence to guide the design of implementation supports (e.g., education, monitoring) needed to achieve those thresholds in diverse patient populations.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard modeling assumption of perfect compliance, explicitly integrating heterogeneous patient behavior as a core determinant of intervention success, which is a critical but under-modeled factor in the decolonization literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Modeling the Impact of Heterogeneous Adherence to Decolonization Protocols on MRSA Transmission Dynamics in Nursing Home Populations
proposals.motivation: Infection control models for multi-drug resistant organisms (MDROs) like MRSA in high-risk settings such as nursing homes often assume perfect, uniform adherence to interventions like chlorhexidine bathing. This assumption is unrealistic and may lead to overestimation of intervention efficacy and flawed policy recommendations. A model incorporating real-world, variable compliance is urgently needed to inform more equitable and effective decolonization strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating empirically-derived, heterogeneous adherence patterns into an agent-based transmission model will reveal non-linear thresholds where suboptimal compliance critically undermines the population-level effectiveness of MRSA decolonization protocols, and that these thresholds differ by participant subgroups.
proposals.method: We will develop a stochastic, agent-based model of MRSA transmission within a nursing home. Adherence to a chlorhexidine/iodophor protocol will be modeled as a time-varying individual trait, with parameters (mean, variance, autocorrelation) derived from prior clinical trial data on participant compliance. We will use the Gillespie algorithm for event-driven simulation. The model will be calibrated and validated against pre-2026 datasets, including colonization outcomes from trials like the one referenced in McKinnell's recent work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel modeling framework that quantifies the relationship between individual-level adherence and population-level infection outcomes for MDROs. It will identify critical compliance thresholds necessary for outbreak prevention, offering concrete targets for stewardship programs and highlighting the need for adherence-supporting interventions alongside biocidal protocols.
proposals.novelty: While previous models have included compliance as a simple, fixed probability, this approach innovates by integrating dynamic, heterogeneous, and empirically-grounded adherence patterns directly into the transmission dynamics, explicitly testing the decolonization 'perfect adherence' assumption that underpins much clinical research.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness of Regional Coordination Versus Facility-Level Control for Multidrug-Resistant Organism (MDRO) Prevention in Nursing Homes: A Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Nursing homes are high-risk reservoirs for MDROs like MRSA, with patient transfers facilitating regional spread. Pre-2026 studies, including McKinnell's work on chlorhexidine bathing, demonstrate effective facility-level interventions, but the economic value of coordinating these efforts across a network of facilities remains unquantified. This gap hinders the development of sustainable, scalable public health strategies to curb the substantial clinical and economic burden of MDRO infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a regionally coordinated infection prevention and control (IPC) strategy, involving synchronized decolonization and surveillance across interconnected nursing homes and hospitals, will be more cost-effective than uncoordinated, facility-level IPC programs.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic, agent-based transmission model simulating MRSA spread within and between nursing homes and a referral hospital in a defined region. The model will incorporate parameters for colonization, infection risk, and patient transfer networks derived from McKinnell's prior clinical research. We will conduct a cost-effectiveness analysis from a healthcare sector perspective, comparing a coordinated regional decolonization protocol (informed by the 'Impact of routine chlorhexidine bathing...' trial) to standard facility-level care. Outcomes will include incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) gained and MDRO colonization events averted.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first economic model quantifying the value of regional IPC coordination for MDROs in post-acute care settings. It will deliver actionable evidence for healthcare administrators and public health officials on efficient resource allocation for infection control. The findings will directly inform policy on whether investing in inter-facility coordination frameworks yields superior population health and economic returns compared to siloed efforts.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses of specific IPC agents (e.g., dalbavancin) exist, applying dynamic transmission modeling to evaluate the economics of *coordination itself* as an intervention in the nursing home network context is novel. This shifts the focus from clinical efficacy of a single drug or protocol to the system-level efficiency of implementing proven interventions.

proposals.title: Integrating Novel Beta-Lactamase Inhibitor Combinations into Agent-Based Models to Project the Long-Term Impact on Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Transmission in Healthcare Networks
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) are a critical public health threat, causing high-mortality infections and spreading efficiently in healthcare settings. While novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam) offer new treatment options, their long-term impact on population-level CRE transmission and resistance evolution remains uncertain. Mathematical models, crucial for stewardship planning, have not yet systematically incorporated the nuanced effects of these agents on colonization clearance, treatment efficacy, and competitive fitness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the pharmacodynamic properties and real-world usage patterns of novel antimicrobial agents into a detailed transmission model will significantly alter projections of CRE prevalence and will identify stewardship strategies that maximize long-term clinical benefit while minimizing the risk of resistance emergence.
proposals.method: We will adapt an existing, validated agent-based model of CRE transmission in a regional healthcare network. Model parameters for novel agents (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam) will be derived from pre-2026 clinical trial data (e.g., clinical cure rates, microbiological eradication) and in vitro pharmacodynamic studies. We will simulate scenarios varying drug deployment strategies (e.g., first-line vs. salvage therapy), duration of use, and adherence to stewardship guidelines. The model will be implemented in Python using the Mesa framework, with outcomes assessed via Monte Carlo simulation to compare CRE colonization prevalence, infection incidence, and resistance emergence over a 5-year horizon against a baseline model with only legacy antibiotics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a dynamic, evidence-based simulation tool to forecast the population-level consequences of new antibiotic introduction. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs by quantifying the trade-offs between immediate patient benefit and long-term resistance selection. The project will yield a methodological framework for rapidly evaluating future antibiotics within transmission models.
proposals.novelty: While previous models have explored antibiotic cycling and mixing, none have been tailored to the specific pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic and resistance profiles of the latest generation of beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations. This proposal uniquely bridges cutting-edge clinical trial data (a strength of McKinnell's recent work) with computational epidemiology to answer a pressing stewardship question.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Chlorhexidine, Octenidine, and Dilute Bleach Bathing on Skin Colonization and Clinical Infection in Nursing Home Residents: A Pragmatic Cluster-Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: Nursing homes are high-risk reservoirs for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like MRSA, leading to severe infections, hospital transfers, and death. While chlorhexidine gluconate (CHG) bathing is a common decolonization strategy, its superiority over other widely available, lower-cost agents like dilute bleach or newer antiseptics like octenidine for routine use in this fragile population is not established. Determining the optimal, safest, and most cost-effective skin antiseptic is crucial for infection prevention and antimicrobial stewardship in long-term care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that routine antiseptic bathing with 2% CHG will be superior to both 0.006% sodium hypochlorite (bleach) and 0.1% octenidine in reducing the composite endpoint of MDRO skin colonization and incident skin and soft tissue infections (SSTIs) among nursing home residents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-arm, pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in 30 nursing homes, matched for size and baseline MDRO prevalence. Homes will be randomized to implement routine bathing with one of the three antiseptic agents for 12 months. The primary outcome is a composite of bi-monthly pooled skin (axilla/groin) swab positivity for MRSA, VRE, or ESBL-producing Gram-negative rods, and electronically-verified incident SSTIs. Secondary outcomes include agent-specific tolerability (dermatitis), cost-effectiveness, and environmental contamination. Analysis will use generalized linear mixed models to account for clustering, with an intention-to-treat approach.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence directly comparing the three most plausible antiseptic bathing regimens for MDRO decolonization in nursing homes. It will generate practical guidance on efficacy, safety, and cost, directly informing infection prevention policies. The findings will advance decolonization research by moving beyond hospital-centric protocols to evidence tailored for the unique epidemiology and participant vulnerabilities of long-term care settings.
proposals.novelty: While CHG bathing has been studied versus standard care, a head-to-head comparison against dilute bleach (a low-cost alternative) and octenidine (a potentially less irritating agent) in nursing homes is novel. This design directly addresses the clinical question of optimal agent selection, integrating decolonization efficacy with key implementation factors like tolerability and cost.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of Nasal Decolonization Agents in Nursing Home Residents: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Trial of Povidone-Iodine, Mupirocin, and Alcohol-Based Antiseptic
proposals.motivation: Nursing homes are high-risk reservoirs for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like MRSA, leading to severe infections, hospital transfers, and significant morbidity. While chlorhexidine bathing is established, the optimal nasal decolonization agent in this setting remains unclear, with mupirocin facing resistance concerns and alcohol-based agents lacking robust long-term data. Identifying the most effective, practical nasal agent is critical for infection prevention in a vulnerable, congregate population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a regimen of nasal povidone-iodine will be superior to nasal mupirocin and to an alcohol-based antiseptic in reducing the prevalence and acquisition of nasal MRSA colonization among nursing home residents over a six-month intervention period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-arm, cluster-randomized, pragmatic trial in 30 nursing homes. Homes will be randomized to one of three nasal decolonization protocols, applied twice daily for five days every two weeks, alongside standard chlorhexidine bathing: 1) 5% povidone-iodine solution, 2) 2% mupirocin ointment, or 3) 62% ethyl alcohol gel. The primary outcome is the change in prevalence of nasal MRSA colonization from baseline to 6 months, measured via bilateral nares swabs processed using chromogenic agar and PCR for *mecA*. Secondary outcomes include MDRO clinical infection rates, agent adherence, and skin tolerance. Analysis will use generalized linear mixed models to account for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first head-to-head comparative effectiveness data for these three nasal decolonization strategies in the nursing home setting. It will directly inform evidence-based guidelines for MDRO prevention in long-term care. The findings will clarify the role of povidone-iodine as a potential resistance-sparing alternative to mupirocin.
proposals.novelty: While prior nursing home studies have combined chlorhexidine bathing with nasal iodophor or mupirocin, no trial has directly compared the efficacy of povidone-iodine against both the standard-of-care (mupirocin) and a commonly used alcohol-based antiseptic in a pragmatic, longitudinal design.

proposals.title: Decolonization to Reduce Hospitalizations for Non-Infectious Complications: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Trial in High-Risk Nursing Home Residents
proposals.motivation: Hospitalizations from nursing homes are frequent, costly, and often driven by non-infectious complications like falls, delirium, and functional decline. Pre-2026 evidence suggests subclinical infection or microbial dysbiosis may be a key, under-recognized driver of these events. Decolonization with chlorhexidine and nasal iodophor reduces MDROs and bloodstream infections, but its potential to prevent hospitalizations by mitigating broader systemic decline remains untested.
proposals.hypothesis: In nursing home residents with a history of MRSA colonization or prior infection, a universal decolonization regimen will reduce the rate of all-cause hospitalization, with a significant proportion of the benefit attributable to the prevention of hospitalizations for non-infectious complications.
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in 40 nursing homes. Facilities will be randomized to intervention (routine chlorhexidine bathing and nasal iodophor for all residents) or standard care. The primary outcome is time to first all-cause hospitalization. Secondary outcomes include hospitalizations specifically for infection vs. non-infectious causes (e.g., falls, delirium), assessed via blinded chart adjudication. We will use mixed-effects Cox proportional hazards models, accounting for clustering by facility and adjusting for baseline resident-level covariates (age, functional status, comorbidities). A prespecified mediation analysis will evaluate the pathway from decolonization to reduced non-infectious hospitalizations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on whether decolonization can prevent hospitalizations beyond its direct anti-infective effect. It will shift the paradigm of decolonization from an infection prevention tool to a broader strategy for stabilizing frail older adults. The findings could directly inform CMS quality measures and infection prevention protocols in long-term care.
proposals.novelty: While decolonization's impact on MDROs and specific infections is established, its effect on systemic, non-infectious complications is a novel and mechanistically distinct outcome. This proposal uniquely tests the hypothesis that reducing microbial burden has pleiotropic benefits, potentially interrupting a key pathway to functional decline and hospitalization.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Genomic and Phenotypic Shifts in Multidrug-Resistant Organisms Following Sustained Decolonization with Chlorhexidine and Povidone-Iodine in Nursing Home Residents
proposals.motivation: Nursing homes are critical reservoirs for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like MRSA, driving healthcare-associated infections. While chlorhexidine bathing and nasal iodophor decolonization reduce MDRO colonization and infection in the short term, their long-term, population-level impact on antimicrobial resistance (AMR) patterns is poorly understood. There is a pre-2026 concern that sustained selective pressure from these biocides may inadvertently promote resistance to clinically important antibiotics or biocides themselves, undermining long-term infection control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that long-term, routine use of chlorhexidine bathing and nasal povidone-iodine in nursing homes will be associated with a quantifiable increase in the prevalence of genetic determinants of biocide tolerance and cross-resistance to antibiotics in colonizing Staphylococcus aureus and other common nursing home pathogens over a 24-month period.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-center, longitudinal cohort study building on prior decolonization trial infrastructure. We will collect serial nasal and inguinal swabs from consenting residents at baseline, 12, and 24 months in facilities with established decolonization protocols. Isolates of S. aureus, Enterococcus spp., and Gram-negative rods will undergo phenotypic susceptibility testing (MICs to chlorhexidine, antibiotics). Whole-genome sequencing will be performed on all isolates to identify known resistance genes (e.g., qacA/B, smr for chlorhexidine; antibiotic resistance determinants) and track phylogenetic evolution. Statistical analysis will use mixed-effects models to associate decolonization exposure duration with changes in resistance gene prevalence and genetic relatedness of strains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, genomic-level evidence of how sustained decolonization shapes the AMR landscape in a high-risk, congregate setting. It will directly inform infection prevention policies by quantifying the potential trade-off between short-term infection reduction and long-term resistance selection. The findings will contribute to a decolonization framework that is clinically effective and ecologically informed.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has assessed clinical outcomes and short-term colonization, this study uniquely integrates longitudinal genomic surveillance with detailed exposure history to directly link decolonization practice to the evolution of resistance mechanisms, moving beyond simple prevalence counts to understand microbial adaptation.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Decolonization Bundle for Reducing Multidrug-Resistant Organism (MDRO) Infections and Associated Resource Use in Post-Acute Care Settings
proposals.motivation: Infections caused by MDROs like MRSA impose a significant clinical and economic burden, particularly in vulnerable populations in nursing homes. While interventions like chlorhexidine bathing and nasal iodophor have shown efficacy in reducing colonization and infection, their widespread adoption is hindered by implementation costs. A rigorous economic evaluation from the healthcare system perspective is needed to inform policy and resource allocation in infection prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a universal decolonization intervention (routine chlorhexidine bathing and nasal iodophor) will be cost-effective compared to standard care in nursing home residents, with the financial benefits from averted infections, reduced antibiotic use (including costly agents like dalbavancin), and decreased hospital transfers outweighing the program's implementation costs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cost-effectiveness analysis using a decision-analytic model (e.g., a Markov model) populated with data from a recent cluster-randomized trial of the intervention. Model parameters will include: intervention costs (supplies, labor), probabilities of MDRO colonization and subsequent infection (derived from trial data), and costs of treating infections (antibiotics, devices, hospitalizations). We will calculate the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) in cost per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) gained and perform deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses to assess robustness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive economic evaluation of a nursing home MDRO decolonization bundle, directly linking infection prevention to healthcare resource utilization. It will offer actionable evidence for administrators and policymakers on the financial viability of such programs, potentially supporting broader implementation to reduce health disparities in post-acute care.
proposals.novelty: While clinical efficacy of decolonization is established, its cost-effectiveness in non-hospital settings is understudied. This analysis uniquely integrates pre-2026 data on MDRO colonization dynamics, real-world antibiotic treatment costs (including novel agents), and the specific resource constraints of nursing homes to address a critical evidence gap in infection prevention economics.

proposals.title: Decolonizing Antibiotic Stewardship: A Mixed-Methods Study to Develop a Clinical Decision Rule for Empiric Antibiotic Initiation in Patients with Suspected MRSA Infection
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship programs often focus on de-escalation or duration of therapy, but the high-stakes decision to initiate empiric anti-MRSA antibiotics remains poorly guided, leading to overuse and collateral damage like resistance and C. difficile. This is particularly critical in settings with high MRSA colonization prevalence, where clinical uncertainty drives unnecessary broad-spectrum use. A decolonization-informed approach, considering patient colonization history and local ecology, could refine this initial decision point.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating pre-admission MRSA colonization status, derived from decolonization program data or prior surveillance, along with specific clinical and demographic risk factors, can accurately identify patients at very low risk for MRSA infection, thereby safely reducing unnecessary empiric anti-MRSA antibiotic initiation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a convergent mixed-methods study. First, a retrospective cohort analysis of adults hospitalized with suspected infection will use multivariable logistic regression to model the association between MRSA infection (outcome) and predictors including prior positive MRSA surveillance culture (nasal/axilla), recent decolonization exposure, healthcare contact, and clinical markers. This will derive a clinical decision rule. Concurrently, qualitative interviews with clinicians and patients will explore perceptions of risk, trust in data, and barriers to rule implementation, ensuring the tool is clinically and ethically actionable.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, pragmatic decision rule to guide the initial antibiotic decision, moving stewardship upstream. It will also provide a novel framework integrating decolonization data into stewardship, offering a model for other MDROs. The mixed-methods design ensures the tool is both statistically sound and contextually grounded for real-world use.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by McKinnell et al. established the impact of decolonization on colonization, this proposal uniquely applies that data stream to the antecedent problem of antibiotic initiation, not review. It merges quantitative predictive modeling with qualitative decolonization principles to create a culturally competent stewardship tool.

proposals.title: Dynamic Modeling of Community-Level Antibiotic Resistance Prevalence to Inform Decolonization Trial Design for MRSA
proposals.motivation: Clinical trials and models for MRSA decolonization (e.g., chlorhexidine/iodophor) often assume a static background prevalence of resistance, ignoring real-world dynamics. The COVID-19 pandemic demonstrated how healthcare disruptions and altered antibiotic use can rapidly shift microbial ecology and resistance patterns. This static assumption may lead to inaccurate estimates of intervention efficacy and cost-effectiveness, particularly in community settings like nursing homes where McKinnell's prior work is situated.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating dynamic, time-varying community prevalence of MRSA and associated antibiotic resistance into transmission models will significantly alter the predicted clinical impact and cost-effectiveness of targeted decolonization strategies compared to models using static prevalence estimates.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based simulation model of MRSA transmission within a network of nursing homes, parameterized with pre-2026 data from McKinnell's trials and surveillance studies. The model will integrate external drivers of resistance prevalence (e.g., seasonal antibiotic prescribing, pandemic-related healthcare strain) as time-series inputs, dynamically modulating the reservoir of resistant strains. We will compare outcomes (e.g., infections averted, resistance selection) from dynamic models against static-baseline models under various decolonization protocols, using sensitivity analysis to identify key drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more realistic modeling framework for evaluating infection prevention interventions in dynamic epidemiological contexts. It will offer practical guidance for designing robust decolonization trials that account for fluctuating resistance pressure, directly informing clinical and public health strategies to combat MRSA.
proposals.novelty: While transmission models of MRSA exist, few integrate real-time, external community drivers to dynamically adjust resistance prevalence within a trial simulation framework. This bridges McKinnell's clinical decolonization research with advanced, dynamic modeling of antimicrobial resistance ecology.

ideas.title: Disentangling General Antiseptic Effects: A Multi-Mechanism, Biomarker-Driven Clinical Trial
ideas.description: This study explicitly tests distinct mechanistic pathways by which a decolonization regimen could provide benefit beyond specific MRSA reduction. The trial randomizes patients to one of three pre-surgical body preparation arms: targeted MRSA decolonization (e.g., mupirocin), broad-spectrum antiseptic (e.g., chlorhexidine), and a control. The primary outcome is a composite of surgical site infections, but the core analysis uses serial quantitative cultures, inflammatory biomarkers (e.g., IL-6), and metagenomic sequencing of the surgical site microbiome to measure changes in total bacterial burden, pathogen transmission, and host inflammatory tone. This design allows causal attribution of any benefit to specific antiseptic mechanisms rather than a monolithic 'general effect'.

ideas.title: Optimizing Behavioral Adherence to Minimize Resistance: A Comparative Trial of Intensive Education vs. Decolonization
ideas.description: This study is a multi-year, cluster-randomized trial comparing a high-intensity, technology-augmented hygiene education program against standard decolonization protocols (e.g., chlorhexidine/mupirocin) in a high-risk population. The primary outcomes are twofold: 1) non-inferiority in preventing target infections, and 2) longitudinal genomic surveillance to quantify and compare the emergence and carriage of antimicrobial resistance genes (e.g., qac, mupA) and multi-drug resistant organisms in each arm. By directly linking behavioral intervention efficacy with resistance selection pressure, the research aims to determine if optimized education can be a viable, sustainable alternative to chemical decolonization, balancing immediate patient safety with long-term antimicrobial stewardship.

ideas.title: Modeling the population-level impact of heterogeneous adherence to universal decolonization at discharge
ideas.description: This research direction develops an agent-based or compartmental transmission model to evaluate the strategy of universal decolonization for all discharged patients. Crucially, it inverts the common assumption of perfect adherence by incorporating realistic, heterogeneous levels of patient compliance with the decolonization protocol post-discharge. The model will quantify how suboptimal and variable adherence affects the overall reduction in community MRSA prevalence and the risk of promoting resistance, compared to both targeted strategies and a theoretical universal strategy with perfect compliance. This allows for the identification of critical adherence thresholds needed for the intervention to be cost-effective and for guiding the design of implementation supports to maximize real-world feasibility and impact.

ideas.title: Targeted Pulse Therapy for Decolonization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the need for a 6-month decolonization regimen by drawing an analogy from antibiotic stewardship in agriculture. In livestock farming, the paradigm shifted from prolonged, low-dose antibiotic use to targeted, short-duration 'pulse therapy' administered only during specific high-risk periods (e.g., weaning), which proved equally effective for disease prevention while reducing resistance. Adapted to this context, the research would test a much shorter, post-discharge-only decolonization 'pulse'—a focused 7-14 day course timed to the immediate post-discharge period when patient vulnerability is highest. This challenges the assumption that duration must be lengthy, proposing instead that precision timing during the peak risk window can achieve equivalent efficacy with a drastically reduced intervention period.

ideas.title: Personalized Decolonization: Adaptive Regimen Design Based on Pre-Treatment Colonization Profiling
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a one-size-fits-all schedule by developing and testing a precision decolonization strategy. The core is a clinical decision support system that uses pre-treatment rapid PCR or genomic sequencing to identify the patient's specific colonization burden, species, and key resistance markers (e.g., mupA, qacA/B). This diagnostic profile, combined with clinical risk factors, feeds into an algorithm that outputs a recommended personalized regimen (choice of agent, duration, and frequency). The approach is validated through a pragmatic randomized controlled trial comparing the clinical and microbiological outcomes of algorithm-guided personalized decolonization against a standard fixed-duration protocol.

ideas.title: Adapting Invasive Species Eradication Protocols for Post-Discharge MDRO Decolonization
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from environmental ecology's approach to eradicating invasive species to the problem of decolonizing carriers of VRE or resistant Gram-negative bacteria post-discharge. Invasive species management solves its analogous problem through sustained, habitat-modifying follow-up after initial containment. The adapted research idea is to design a clinical trial where discharged carriers receive a multi-modal 'eradication protocol.' This protocol would include: 1) an initial decolonization treatment, 2) scheduled at-home self-collection of swabs for molecular surveillance, 3) on-demand, short-course decolonization triggered only by a positive surveillance result, and 4) provision of EPA-registered disinfectants and instructions for concurrent decontamination of the patient's home environment. The goal is to evaluate if this ecological, habitat-focused maintenance strategy is more effective than a single post-discharge intervention at achieving long-term decolonization and preventing transmission.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Predict Pathogen Emergence Post-MRSA Decolonization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that targeted decolonization of a single pathogen like MRSA is without broader ecological risk. By drawing an analogy to community ecology, where the removal of a keystone species can lead to unexpected shifts in community structure, we propose a novel framework. The analogous solution from ecology involves using niche theory and competition models to predict which species fill a vacated niche. Adapted to this medical context, the research would systematically characterize the ecological niche of MRSA within the host microbiome and immune environment. Using this data, combined with in vitro and in vivo models of decolonization, the study would test predictions about which specific pathogens (e.g., Enterococcus faecalis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, or Candida auris) are 'pre-adapted' to exploit the newly available resources and space, thereby identifying patients at highest risk for subsequent infections from these alternative pathogens.

ideas.title: Optimized, Source-Targeted Decolonization: Using Inverse Modeling to Guide Short-Course Intervention
ideas.description: This research combines the concepts of shortened decolonization regimens and inverse transmission modeling to develop a precision infection prevention strategy. The core idea is to use an inverse modeling approach on observed CRE colonization patterns within a healthcare network to pinpoint the most probable sources and routes of ongoing transmission. Instead of applying a standard 6-month or even a generic short-course decolonization to all discharged patients, the model's output is used to stratify patients. Only those identified as high-risk for reintroduction or perpetuation—based on their connection to modeled sources and pathways—receive a targeted, evidence-based short-course decolonization immediately post-discharge. This synergy allows for testing whether a resource-efficient, highly focused intervention can disrupt transmission networks as effectively as a broader, longer-duration protocol.

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Model Parameter Perturbation Methods to Assess Novel Antibiotic Impact on CRE Projections
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to integrate the potential effect of novel antimicrobial agents into Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) transmission simulations by adapting a solution from climate science: the perturbed parameter ensemble (PPE) method. In climate modeling, PPEs are used to quantify how uncertainties in new forcing agents (like geoengineering technologies) affect long-term projections. The analogous solution involves defining the key uncertain properties of the new agent and running the model across a wide range of those parameters. Adapted to this context, the research would identify and parameterize the critical uncertainties surrounding a future antibiotic (e.g., efficacy, rollout speed, resistance development). By running the CRE simulation as a Monte Carlo ensemble that samples from these defined parameter distributions, the work would generate a probabilistic assessment of how the new drug could alter transmission trajectories, providing a more robust and uncertainty-aware projection than a single deterministic scenario.

ideas.title: Predictive Genomic Surveillance and Adaptive Cycling to Preempt Resistance in Decolonization Regimens
ideas.description: This study implements a closed-loop system that moves beyond passive monitoring to actively prevent resistance. It employs longitudinal, deep whole-genome sequencing of Staphylococcus aureus populations from patients undergoing decolonization, focusing on detecting low-frequency variants and gene expression changes associated with adaptive stress responses. When predictive biomarkers of potential resistance (e.g., efflux pump upregulation, mutations in fabI) are identified, the protocol automatically triggers a switch to a pre-defined alternative agent (e.g., povidone-iodine, octenidine) for the next cycle. This adaptive, multi-agent approach aims to suppress resistance emergence by removing the constant selective pressure of a single agent.

ideas.title: Network-Optimized Economic Analysis for Regional Decolonization Strategy
ideas.description: This research synthesizes economic evaluation and network science to develop a dynamic model for post-discharge decolonization. It uses network centrality measures to identify high-impact facilities where decolonization would most effectively prevent regional transmission through patient transfers. The model then performs a cost-effectiveness analysis that accounts for both the direct benefits at the targeted facility and the downstream, network-wide savings from prevented infections and hospitalizations across the connected system. This integrated approach allows for the optimization of decolonization investments to maximize the economic return and public health impact for the entire regional network, rather than for individual hospitals in isolation.

ideas.title: Modeling Variable Protocol Adherence Using Pharmacokinetic Compliance Frameworks
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the common assumption of perfect adherence to infection control protocols in epidemiological models. By drawing an analogy from pharmacology—where pharmacokinetic models successfully handle variable patient adherence to medication regimens—we propose a novel modeling framework. The analogous solution involves treating protocol adherence as a time-varying, stochastic parameter that modulates the 'dose' of intervention received, similar to how drug concentration profiles are modeled with imperfect compliance. The adapted idea applies this PK/PD-inspired framework to infection spread, modeling the 'effective intervention dose' (e.g., from mask use, distancing) as a function of empirically observed, suboptimal human compliance patterns. This allows for dynamic simulation of outbreak trajectories under realistic, heterogeneous adherence, providing more accurate predictions of intervention effectiveness and identifying critical adherence thresholds for outbreak control.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Theory to Model Decolonization Investment Risk and Return
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from financial economics—Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)—to the problem of assessing the cost-effectiveness of post-discharge decolonization. In finance, MPT constructs an 'efficient frontier' to optimize the balance between an investment's expected return and its associated risk. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing different decolonization protocols (varying by agent, duration, and target patient group) as a portfolio of investment options for a healthcare system. The research would conduct a formal economic analysis that models the 'return' (net savings from prevented infections and hospitalizations) against the 'risk' (statistical variance in outcomes due to adherence, resistance, or patient heterogeneity). The output would be an 'efficient frontier' of strategy combinations, providing a decision-making framework to select the decolonization portfolio that offers the optimal risk-adjusted cost-effectiveness for a given health system's tolerance for outcome variability.

ideas.title: A Community-Based, Targeted 'Source Control' Strategy for MRSA Decolonization
ideas.description: This research adapts the principle of 'source control' from agricultural integrated pest management (IPM) to MRSA decolonization in community-dwelling carriers. The analogous IPM solution avoids broad interventions by identifying and targeting specific pest reservoirs. Adapted here, the study will evaluate a personalized decolonization strategy. After identifying community-dwelling MRSA carriers, detailed screening will map their personal reservoirs (e.g., household environment, cohabitants, pets). Effectiveness will then be tested for a tailored intervention package precisely targeting those identified reservoirs, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all patient-centric protocol to an ecological, source-focused approach.

ideas.title: Targeted, Data-Driven Hygiene Education to Disrupt Outpatient and Pediatric Transmission Networks
ideas.description: This study integrates detailed epidemiological mapping of pathogen transmission in outpatient clinics and pediatric populations with a dynamic, technology-supported hygiene education platform. The intervention uses the collected transmission data to identify specific high-touch surfaces, common behavioral lapses, and peak transmission times unique to these settings, then delivers targeted, interactive educational content (e.g., just-in-time gamified reminders, visual feedback on high-risk behaviors) precisely where and when the data indicates risk is highest. The core innovation is testing whether this data-driven, context-specific education can match the efficacy of universal decolonization by disrupting the most critical transmission pathways identified in real-time, thereby offering a more scalable and acceptable precision public health strategy.

ideas.title: Mechanistic, phenotype-anchored modeling of strain-dependent CRE transmission
ideas.description: This research integrates experimental microbiology with mathematical modeling to create a causally grounded framework for variable pathogen transmissibility. First, high-throughput phenotypic assays quantify key transmission-relevant traits (e.g., environmental persistence, host colonization efficiency) for a diverse panel of CRE strains. Machine learning models then map genetic features to these measured phenotypes, creating a predictive genotype-to-phenotype pipeline. These experimentally derived phenotypic estimates are used to inform and constrain the transmission parameters in a hierarchical epidemiological model of hospital outbreaks, ensuring that the transmissibility estimates are mechanistically interpretable and independently testable outside of outbreak fitting.

ideas.title: Dynamic Intervention Efficacy: A Closed-Loop Surveillance and Adaptive Response System
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simply acknowledging that intervention efficacy can change, to actively and continuously measuring it through integrated data streams. It employs a real-time Bayesian inference model to synthesize data on clinical outcomes, staff behavioral audits, and pathogen genomics, producing a live estimate of effectiveness with credible intervals. When the model detects a statistically significant decline in efficacy, it automatically initiates a root-cause analysis and deploys a corresponding adaptive response, such as targeted staff retraining or a shift to an alternative intervention protocol, thereby closing the loop between detection and action.

ideas.title: Bayesian Uncertainty Quantification for Probabilistic Source Attribution of Endemic Pathogens
ideas.description: This research develops a robust source attribution system for pathogens like CRE by embedding the inverse epidemiological model within a Bayesian hierarchical framework. It integrates prior knowledge from genomic sequencing (phylogenetic relationships), patient transfer networks, and local antibiotic usage to constrain the ill-posed problem. The output is a spatially explicit posterior probability distribution over potential introduction points, rigorously quantifying the uncertainty in source identification. This approach transforms the original deterministic goal into a probabilistic inference, enabling public health decisions based on risk rather than a single, potentially misleading, answer.

ideas.title: Dynamic Resilience Modeling for Healthcare Supply-Demand Networks
ideas.description: This research synthesizes supply chain resilience frameworks with dynamic healthcare contact networks to model and mitigate cascading failures in infection control. It develops an integrated simulation where disruptions in resource distribution (e.g., PPE, antivirals) are dynamically influenced by and, in turn, influence shifting patient referral patterns and facility capacity constraints over time. This allows for the identification of critical, time-sensitive vulnerabilities where supply shortages and patient surge converge, threatening network-wide infection prevention. The outcome is a decision-support tool for designing adaptive resource stockpiling and distribution policies that are responsive to the evolving state of both the healthcare network and its supply logistics.

ideas.title: Modeling Dynamic Intervention Efficacy with Adaptive Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of fixed intervention efficacy over time by adapting a solution from ecological pest management. In that field, dynamic feedback models monitor control agent performance and pest population adaptation, triggering strategic shifts to maintain control. Adapted to a healthcare context, the research would develop a modeling framework where the effectiveness of an intervention (e.g., an antimicrobial protocol) can wane due to pathogen adaptation or improve through staff training. The model integrates real-time data streams (e.g., resistance markers, compliance audits) to create feedback loops that signal when efficacy is drifting from baseline. This allows for the pre-emptive activation of contingency plans or adaptive measures, moving from static, time-blind evaluation to a dynamic, responsive management system for interventions.

ideas.title: Model CRE endemicity as a managed ecosystem using ecological stability theory
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from theoretical ecology for modeling long-term species persistence under management to the problem of CRE endemicity. The analogous problem is predicting whether a species (or pathogen) will become endemic, be eradicated, or oscillate under sustained control efforts, solved using ecological stability theory and bifurcation analysis. The adapted research idea simulates CRE spread over decades by conceptualizing the healthcare-community environment as a managed ecosystem. Different CRE strains and patient/reservoir populations are treated as interacting species, while sustained interventions (infection control, stewardship) are modeled as continuous management pressures. The goal is to use resilience theory and identify alternative stable states and critical thresholds to predict the long-term (10+ year) dynamics of CRE under various sustained control regimes, moving beyond short-term outbreak models to understand the conditions for stable, low-level endemicity.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Backtracking for CRE Source Identification
ideas.description: This idea adapts phylogenetic backtracking from evolutionary biology to solve the inverse problem in CRE epidemiology. The analogous solution infers the geographic origin of a species by analyzing the diversity and structure of its current population phylogeny. Adapted here, we use a computational model of CRE transmission within a healthcare network. The model's parameters (like transmission rates, patient movement) are informed by real data. Given an observed, stable (endemic) distribution of CRE cases and strain types across the network, we run the model in reverse using statistical inversion methods (e.g., Bayesian source attribution or likelihood optimization). This identifies the most probable initial introduction node(s) and timing within the network that, when forward-simulated through the known contact structure, would result in the observed endemic pattern. This provides a data-driven method to pinpoint historical outbreak sources from current surveillance data.

ideas.title: Incorporate multi-scale, sentinel surveillance data from outpatient and pediatric settings using ecological network methods
ideas.description: This research expands the model to include transmission pathways in outpatient settings and pediatric populations by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, scientists map disease spread in complex, fragmented landscapes using sentinel species and sensor networks, building models that infer connections between observed nodes. Adapting this solution, we propose treating outpatient clinics, pediatric offices, and urgent care centers as sentinel nodes within a broader healthcare ecosystem. The adapted idea is to develop a multi-scale, sentinel surveillance network model that integrates often sparse, real-time data from these non-hospital settings (such as syndromic reports, antiviral prescriptions, or rapid test results) with core hospital data. This model will use network inference techniques from ecology to reconstruct hidden transmission pathways, explicitly quantifying the contribution of outpatient and pediatric transmission to overall epidemic dynamics, thereby addressing a critical blind spot in existing healthcare-associated infection models.

ideas.title: Apply Network Contagion Models from Financial Systems to CRE Spread
ideas.description: This idea adapts network contagion models from financial economics, specifically those used to analyze systemic risk and shock propagation in interbank lending networks, to the problem of CRE spread in healthcare systems. The analogous solution maps financial exposures between institutions to epidemiological connections (e.g., patient transfer patterns, shared personnel) between semi-isolated healthcare facilities. The adapted research idea involves constructing a directed, weighted network of healthcare facilities, applying algorithms like DebtRank—originally designed to quantify the systemic importance of financial institutions—to quantify the 'outbreak risk' contribution of each facility. This allows for the simulation of pathogen spread through the network to identify critical control points and predict the cascading effects of an outbreak in one facility on the broader healthcare ecosystem, moving beyond traditional metapopulation models to incorporate complex, asymmetric network dynamics.

ideas.title: Applying Forest Fire Containment Models to Single-Facility Outbreak Resource Allocation
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from wildland fire management to the problem of internal resource allocation during a single-facility Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) outbreak. In fire management, dynamic models optimally position limited crews and equipment along a moving fire front based on real-time spread dynamics to minimize total cost (resource expenditure + property loss). Analogously, we apply the RHEA framework to model a CRE outbreak as a dynamic 'infection front' spreading within a hospital. The adapted idea is to develop an optimization model that treats limited internal resources (isolation capacity, specialized staff, cleaning) as 'firefighting units,' deploying them not uniformly, but to specific, high-priority 'containment lines' (e.g., ICU, surgical units) in real-time. The goal is to optimally allocate these scarce resources to interrupt transmission pathways most effectively, thereby minimizing the total cost of the outbreak, which includes both the resources consumed for control and the negative health outcomes incurred.

ideas.title: Adapting 'Flipped Classroom' and Gamified Learning from STEM Education to Test a High-Engagement Hygiene Education Intervention
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that physical decolonization is necessary by proposing a maximally intensive educational alternative, drawing an analogy from solutions in STEM education pedagogy. In STEM, moving from passive lectures to active, technology-supported models like the 'flipped classroom' and gamified learning significantly improved engagement and outcomes. We adapt this solution by developing a hygiene education intervention where participants first complete interactive, gamified digital modules on hygiene principles and microbial transmission. This is followed by facilitated group sessions dedicated to practicing skills, solving real-world contamination scenarios, and peer discussion. The research would test if this high-engagement, technology-supported educational program can match the infection reduction outcomes of the decolonization group, thereby isolating and evaluating the potential of education alone when delivered with optimal, evidence-based pedagogical methods.

ideas.title: Modeling Infection Control Coordination as a Multi-Agent Resource Allocation Game
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from telecommunications network management to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of coordinated infection control. In wireless networks, the problem of interference management between uncoordinated base stations is solved using game theory, comparing decentralized Nash equilibria to centralized optimization. Adapted to public health, healthcare facilities are modeled as agents in a non-cooperative game making independent infection control investments, which create spillover effects. The study would formally construct this game, compute the cost and infection outcomes at the Nash equilibrium (uncoordinated control) and under a hypothetical regional planner's optimal allocation (coordinated control). The core economic analysis would quantify the 'price of anarchy' (the efficiency gap) and weigh it against the practical transaction costs of establishing and maintaining a regional coordination body, providing a rigorous framework for policy decision-making.

ideas.title: Modeling pathogen transmission as an environmental contaminant with diffuse community reservoirs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of no community transmission by drawing an analogy from environmental engineering's approach to groundwater contamination. Just as hydrologists moved beyond modeling single point-source plumes to account for multiple, diffuse contaminant sources (like agricultural runoff), epidemiological models can relax the assumption of transmission solely linked to healthcare facilities. The analogous solution—integrating distributed sensor data, land-use maps, and hydrological models—is adapted by using syndromic surveillance, anonymized mobility data, and environmental pathogen sampling (e.g., wastewater) to characterize the community as a landscape with diffuse, ongoing transmission reservoirs. The resulting adapted research idea is to develop a transmission model that explicitly maps and quantifies these background community pathways, providing a more accurate foundation for assessing the relative contribution of healthcare-associated spread.

ideas.title: A Modular, Context-Aware Framework for Data-Sparse Transmission Settings
ideas.description: This research develops a unified modeling framework to integrate transmission pathways across diverse outpatient and pediatric environments. The core innovation is a modular structure that separates context-specific contact patterns (derived from targeted behavioral studies and environmental sensing) from a general pathogen transmission engine. The framework employs a tiered data assimilation system, prioritizing high-quality sentinel site data to calibrate models for broader, data-sparse settings, ensuring robustness despite heterogeneous data availability. This approach generates transferable, evidence-based transmission kernels for these critical but under-modeled community interfaces.

ideas.title: Dynamic Network-Centric Optimization for Adaptive Healthcare Surveillance
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network centrality optimization with dynamic network modeling to create an adaptive framework for healthcare surveillance. It models the regional healthcare facility network as a temporal graph, where edges (referral patterns) and node attributes (capacities) evolve over time. By applying time-dependent centrality measures, the method identifies facilities that are consistently critical or become transiently critical during specific periods, such as seasonal outbreaks or capacity shortages. This allows for the optimization of targeted surveillance and resource pre-positioning strategies that are resilient to the inherent dynamism of healthcare systems, improving outbreak detection and response efficacy.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Trophic Network Stability Analysis to Infection Control Supply Chains
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from ecology. The analogous problem is understanding the stability and collapse risk of complex ecological networks like food webs, which is solved by modeling species interactions, identifying keystone species, and simulating extinction events to assess systemic vulnerability. This solution is adapted to the context of infection control resource distribution by conceptualizing the supply chain as an ecological network. Nodes represent entities (manufacturers, distributors, hospitals), and links represent the flow of critical resources. By applying ecological stability metrics and disruption simulations from trophic network analysis, the research will identify high-risk, non-redundant pathways and critical nodes. The outcome is a predictive model for resource shortage cascades and evidence-based principles for designing more resilient, modular, and redundant supply networks for infection control, directly transferring a methodological framework from ecology to public health logistics.

ideas.title: Adapting Traffic Flow Agent-Based Models for Healthcare Transmission Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts agent-based modeling (ABM) solutions from the field of transportation science, specifically urban traffic flow simulation, to the problem of simulating healthcare-associated pathogen transmission. In traffic ABMs, individual vehicles (agents) follow behavioral rules within a network, generating complex system-level outcomes like congestion. The analogous solution is adapted by modeling healthcare workers and patients as agents navigating a hospital 'network' (wards, hallways, common areas). Their behaviors—governed by rules for hygiene, proximity, and movement—interact to produce emergent transmission dynamics. This adapted ABM framework allows for in-silico testing of intervention strategies, such as altering staff rotation patterns or triage pathways, to reduce infection risk, mirroring how traffic models test signal changes to reduce congestion.
