ideas.title: Closed-Loop Epidemiologically-Informed Resource Allocation (CLERA) System
ideas.description: This research designs a hybrid modeling system that tightly couples real-time epidemiological outbreak simulations with adaptive supply chain optimization for infection control resources. The core innovation is using pathogen transmission dynamics and surveillance data to generate probabilistic, geospatial demand forecasts, which then directly constrain a multi-echelon distribution network model capable of dynamic prepositioning and redistribution. This creates a proactive, closed-loop decision-support tool that moves beyond static resilience frameworks to one where the supply network continuously adapts to the predicted evolution of an epidemic, minimizing response lag and critical shortages in hotspots.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Predict Toolkit Efficacy Across Healthcare Ecosystems
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Conservation Biology, where Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) is used to predict species distributions. The analogous problem is determining where a conservation strategy proven in one ecosystem will work in another. The solution involves modeling the 'niche'—the combination of environmental conditions—of the successful intervention. Adapted to this research context, we would first analyze the Orange County RHEA simulations to identify the precise combination of healthcare system variables (the 'intervention niche') correlated with high CDC toolkit impact. This niche model, defined by factors like facility types, reimbursement structures, and population health indices, would then be projected onto geographic and organizational datasets for other U.S. regions. The output would be a predictive suitability map ranking regions by how closely their healthcare ecosystems match the proven success niche from Orange County, thereby creating a targeted, hypothesis-driven framework for selecting the most promising new regions for simulation testing.

ideas.title: Robust intervention portfolios for CRE endemicity under deep structural uncertainty
ideas.description: This research moves beyond single-model long-term projection by developing an ensemble of alternative computational models that embody fundamentally different assumptions about CRE evolution, host ecology, and healthcare infrastructure over multi-decade timescales. Using scenario discovery and robustness analysis techniques, the system stress-tests various sustained intervention bundles against this ensemble to identify which strategies avoid worst-case outcomes across the broadest set of plausible futures. The final output is not a precise forecast, but a map of the conditions under which different policies fail or succeed, and a set of adaptive, robust intervention portfolios prioritized for long-term resilience against deep uncertainty.

ideas.title: Testing the Necessity of Environmental Enablement for Education-Driven Hygiene Behavior Change
ideas.description: This study employs a 2x2 factorial randomized controlled trial to dismantle the assumption that education delivery is the sole critical factor. It independently manipulates the provision of an intensive, interactive hygiene education program and the concurrent provision of essential environmental supports (e.g., consistent soap supply, handwashing station repairs). The primary outcome is a validated measure of observed hygiene behavior. This design will conclusively determine if enhanced education can match decolonization outcomes on its own, or if its effectiveness is fundamentally limited without addressing structural barriers, thereby providing a more robust and actionable evidence base for intervention design.

ideas.title: Resilience-adaptive healthcare contact network integrating shock detection and crisis response dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid modeling system for healthcare facility networks that operates on two interconnected tiers. The first tier continuously updates a dynamic model of routine patient flow and capacity changes. The second, resilience tier is activated by real-time anomaly detection algorithms that identify systemic shocks from data streams. Upon detection, the model incorporates crisis-response dynamics, simulating adaptive behaviors like triage protocol shifts, temporary facility role changes, and emergency resource allocation to project network performance and identify failure points under extreme stress.

ideas.title: Dynamic Mechanism Design with Stability Credits for Robust Regional Health Surveillance
ideas.description: This research designs a dynamic incentive system for inter-facility health surveillance that overcomes the instability of static cooperative agreements. It employs a mechanism design framework, fed by real-time data on testing capacity and outbreak risk, to calculate and allocate tradable stability credits to facilities based on their verified participation and data-sharing performance. This creates a transparent market for cooperation, where facilities are directly compensated for their burden, ensuring sustained engagement even when priorities conflict. The system is rigorously tested via simulation against historical data to prove its robustness against strategic defection and shifting operational conditions.

ideas.title: National Simulation of Regional Coordination Incentives for CRE Control
ideas.description: This research develops a national-scale, agent-based simulation model to evaluate how federal policies (e.g., grants, penalties, shared liability frameworks) can be designed to overcome economic barriers and incentivize regional coordination for CRE control. It moves beyond comparing costs to actively testing financial and regulatory mechanisms that make coordination the most economically attractive strategy for individual facilities. The model will simulate facility behaviors under different policy scenarios to identify the federal levers most effective at catalyzing sustainable, cost-effective coordination networks across the heterogeneous U.S. healthcare landscape.

ideas.title: Adapting Coalitional Game Theory from International Fisheries Management for Regional Health Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea adapts a proven solution from international fisheries management—where nations cooperate to sustainably manage a shared resource—to the problem of incentivizing healthcare facility participation in regional disease surveillance. In fisheries, coalitional game theory models nations as players and uses concepts like the Shapley value to allocate fishing quotas fairly based on contribution, stabilizing cooperation. Adapted here, healthcare facilities (hospitals, clinics, labs) are modeled as players in a cooperative game where the shared resource is the regional surveillance intelligence generated from pooled data. An algorithmic framework based on the Shapley value would be designed to dynamically allocate rewards from a shared regional fund or provide non-monetary benefits (e.g., advanced analytics reports, diagnostic capacity support) to each facility. The allocation is proportional to the facility's marginal contribution to key network metrics like population coverage, pathogen detection speed, and data completeness. This creates a transparent, equitable economic and operational incentive for facilities to join and actively maintain the surveillance coalition, moving beyond voluntary participation or top-down mandates.

ideas.description: This research designs a simulation-driven framework that moves beyond static centrality to optimize surveillance for resilience. It models the regional network as a dynamic system where flows, node functionalities, and adversary strategies can change in response to disruptions or surveillance itself. By stress-testing the network under a range of failure and adaptation scenarios, the system identifies surveillance portfolios that maintain coverage and coordination effectiveness over time, ensuring robustness against both random shocks and intelligent circumvention.

ideas.title: Modeling Healthcare Contact Networks as Dynamic Traffic Flow Systems
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the static assumption in healthcare facility contact networks by incorporating dynamic changes in referral patterns and capacities. Drawing an analogy from Transportation Engineering, specifically Dynamic Traffic Assignment models used for urban traffic flow, the solution adapts the concept of time-varying network performance and user equilibrium. The adapted idea models patient referrals as dynamic flows, using time-dependent link performance functions to represent evolving facility capacities and referral latencies, thereby creating a simulation framework where referral 'traffic' is continuously reassigned based on real-time system state, mirroring how drivers choose routes in response to congestion.

ideas.title: Apply Network Resilience Metrics from Ecology to Optimize Surveillance Facility Selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology, where network science is used to prioritize habitat patches for conservation based on their role in connectivity and system resilience. The analogous solution uses centrality combined with simulations of network fragmentation to identify keystone patches. Adapted to the original context of optimizing coordination for targeted surveillance, the research would involve modeling the regional facility network and applying ecological resilience metrics (e.g., node deletion impact, bridging centrality, modularity change) to identify facilities whose surveillance would most effectively enhance the network's overall robustness to disruptions or disease spread, moving beyond static centrality to a dynamic, system-preserving strategy.
